Upper Valley Events

Scraped from home.dartmouth.edu/events · nhhumanities.org · avagallery.org · shakermuseum.org · nugget-theaters.com · entertainmentcinemas.com/lebanon-6

from July 24 to October 22, 2026.

Friday, July 24, 2026

10:00 am - 11:30 am China, the United States, and the Challenges of a New World Order Osher at Dartmouth 1 Court St. Suite 210 Lebanon, NH 03766

The development of China as an economic and political powerhouse has generated serious concerns in Washington and the West in general. The view that, as a developing nation, China would morph into some sort of capitalist democracy was assumed. This was a profound miscalculation. In fact all the norms of the "Washington Consensus," the western rules-based order, are now being challenged. Join Osher at Dartmouth in Lebanon to examine the evolution of this "rules-based order" driven by western interests and how the dynamics of 3rd world nations (referred to as “the Global South") and China challenge the post World War II western consensus.

Richard Price is a former public relations professional. He has written, published, and presented on a broad range of topics including political, historical, and technical subjects. His decades-long interest in China and the developing world, including his travels to China and Asia, has given him a perspective and deep appreciation of a changing world and the challenges facing western society in coping. He has shared this perspective with many groups, providing insight into evolving geopolitical matters.

COST:
Osher Members - $10
Non-Members - $15

LEARN MORE AND REGISTER

Osher Lifelong Learning Institute at Dartmouth
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
12:30 pm - 1:30 pm Writing After Dartmouth: Panel Discussion Literary Arts Bridge (7 Lebanon St. Suite 105)

Ask a stellar panel of recent Dartmouth alumnae about grad school, writing, publishing, and navigating the years after college. This panel discussion will be moderated by Professor Alexander Chee.

A light lunch will be provided. Registration encouraged. Please email laura.jean.gilloux@dartmouth.edu.

About the Panelists

Carolyn Silverstein ’21 earned her MFA in Creative Writing from Colorado State University and her BA in English Literature and Creative Writing from Dartmouth College. Her writing has appeared in Archipelago, Vol. III, Stonefence Review, the Write Launch, and McNeese Review. Her work has been shortlisted for the Virginia Woolf Award in Short Fiction. A native New Yorker, Carolyn is a certified ski instructor and enjoys spending time with her dog, Gatsby, when she’s not writing.

Allison Hufford ’21 is a graduate of Dartmouth College, where she majored in English literature and minored in environmental studies.  As JRA’s Subsidiary Rights Manager, she oversees the licensing of translation, audio, electronic, book club, and large print rights throughout the world in over fifty languages. She and her team work tirelessly in collaboration with a highly skilled network of foreign agents and scouts to market JRA authors and their work internationally, including in person at the London and Frankfurt book fairs. She is an avid reader with a particular love for character-driven narratives, lyrical writing styles, and anything with a speculative twist.  

Elizabeth Janowski ’21 (she/her) graduated from Dartmouth with a double major in history and film and media studies and served as executive editor of The Dartmouth. She received an M.S. in journalism from Columbia University in 2022. Elizabeth is an assistant editor at Viking Penguin, where she has worked with authors including Amor Towles, Tana French, Alejandro Varela, Ruth Ozeki, Nathaniel Philbrick, and Maggie Stiefvater. Originally from Milwaukee, Elizabeth lives in New York City. 

About the Moderator

Alexander Chee is the best-selling author of the novels The Queen of the Night and Edinburgh, and the essay collection How to Write an Autobiographical Novel. He is a contributing editor at The New Republic, and an editor at large at The Virginia Quarterly Review. He is the recipient of a 2021 Guggenheim Fellowship and a 2021 United States Artists Fellowship, and awards and fellowships from the Whiting Foundation and the NEA, and residency fellowships from the MacDowell Colony, the VCCA, Civitella Ranieri and Amtrak. He is a professor of English and Creative Writing at Dartmouth College and the chair of the Dartmouth Literary Arts Bridge.

Literary Arts Bridge
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
4:00 pm - 4:45 pm A SPACE FOR DIALOGUE GALLERY TALK Hood Museum of Art

All dates: Jul 24, Oct 1

I Remember Who I Am When I’m with You

The museum’s Class of 1954 Intern Madeleine Saraisky ’26 will discuss her student-curated exhibition that examines a range of photographs and prints that depict many types of relationships to explore the idea of interpersonal witnessing as a worthwhile action, one that shapes the course of our lives and allows us to impact others in return.

Photographs may be taken at this event for promotional or educational use. By attending, you consent to be photographed and/or video recorded. Please contact us if you have any questions or concerns.

Hood Museum of Art
Full details ↗
Jul 24 Oct 1
5 pm - 6 pm New York Theatre Workshop: Artist-in-Residence Salon Hopkins Center

Beyond the works presented each Saturday, NYTW invites artists to Dartmouth for a week of studio time. Come hear from these artists-in-residence in an Open Studio setting about the new works they are dreaming up.

Join us in the Morris Recital Hall for the start of the Artist Salons, which will take place throughout the Hopkins Center.

Mystical Tether
Co-created and Composed by Britton Smith
Co-created by Satchel Lee
A new music-theatre work in early development centered on a queer Black grandson and his grandmother. The piece unfolds inside a single house—both literal and psychological—representing belonging, early lessons, comfort and distorted memory.

The Dispute
Created by Michael Breslin
The Dispute is a new musical inspired by Marivaux's 1742 comedy La Dispute. In a fairytale world, a prince and princess enter a drag-out dispute: Who was first to be unfaithful in love, man or woman? A social experiment unfurls. Four young adults, raised in total isolation from birth, are released into the wild to test the origins of love, sex and betrayal. But when one subject's desire starts to outgrow the limits of the experiment, identities and alliances shift, and the whole thing goes dangerously––and gleefully––off the rails.

This event is free but ticketed. Registration is recommended.

Get more info here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7 pm - 9 pm Blade Runner (DFS: Killer Robots!) Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Harrison Ford hunts down rogue androids, nearly indistinguishable from humans, in Ridley Scott's masterful 1982 sci-fi noir adaptation of Philip K. Dick's story.

Tasked with hunting down a set of rogue androids, "Blade Runner" Rick Deckard's (Harrison Ford) mission is simple—but the issues it raises are anything but. It covers notions of sentience, empathy and human rights, with iconic supporting performances, unmatched production design and a dreamy synth score. 

One of the most influential films of the past 40 years, a dazzling fusion of retro-noir and future paranoia, set in a decaying, rain-drenched Los Angeles dominated by Mayan pyramids of unimaginable wealth. This "Final Cut" is the most complete version of this undisputed masterpiece, representing 25 years of finessing Scott's vision—a cinematic experience not to be missed.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Jun 18 – Aug 15, 2026 World Repair: Recent Paintings by John Stomberg Linda Roesch Visual Arts Gallery | AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 11:00 am · Free

AVA Gallery and Art Center is pleased to present, "World Repair: Recent Paintings by John Stomberg." From potholes to the justice system, Stomberg sees a world in need of repair. Obsessed with the question "Can we fix the world?", he has now channeled years of artistic inquiry into paintings that explore the ancient Jewish concept of Tikkun Olam, or world repair, through a vivid visual language of sweeping gold lines, expressive geometry, and radiant color. The result of his last few years of exploration are deeply hopeful paintings that ask what it means to mend what is broken, in both the earthly and spiritual sense. To view John's online gallery Click here, all proceeds will be generously donated to AVA!

John Stomberg holds a B.A. in Art History from Georgetown University and a Ph.D. in Art History from Boston University. His distinguished career in museum leadership has included positions at the Boston University Art Gallery, the Williams College Museum of Art, the Mount Holyoke College Art Museum, and most recently as the Virginia Rice Kelsey 1961s Director of the Hood Art Museum at Dartmouth College, from which he will soon retire.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Jun 29 – Aug 10, 2026 Call to Artists: A Place Visited Only In Dreams Linda Roesch Visual Arts Gallery, Gallery 3 | AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 6:00 am

a place visited only in dreams | call to artists!

Submission Deadline: Mon. Aug. 10. Exhibition dates: Sept. 25-Nov. 14, 2026. Click here to submit your artwork

**Call for Artwork in the Style of Surrealism** A Place Visited Only in Dreams invites artists to explore the shifting terrain of the subconscious, where logic dissolves and imagination reigns. Rooted in the legacy of Surrealism, this exhibition seeks works that construct alternate realities—worlds shaped by dream imagery, memory fragments, intuition, and the irrational. Surrealism has long offered a visual language for expressing what cannot be spoken directly: desire, fear, wonder, longing, and the strange poetry of the mind at rest. In this exhibition, we are drawn to artworks that embrace unexpected juxtapositions, uncanny atmospheres, and illogical pairings that provoke both unease and fascination. Whether playful or haunting, meditative or disruptive, these works invite viewers to step into spaces that feel familiar yet impossible— places visited only in dreams.

We welcome submissions in all media that interpret dream states, altered perception, subconscious narrative, or imagined worlds. Artists are encouraged to consider how their work might open a portal to the irrational, the symbolic, or the deeply personal terrain of inner vision.

Step beyond waking reality. Enter the dream.

Details: Submission Deadline: Mon. Aug. 10. Exhibition dates: Sept. 25-Nov. 14, 2026. Opening Reception: Friday, Sept. 25, 5-7 PM. Artwork Delivery: Tues. Sept. 8, 11-2PM. Artwork Pickup: Tues. Nov. 17. Shipping: UPS only! Must include a paid shipping return label. Must arrive by September 4. Attention: SAM

This is a group, invitational, curated exhibition. Unfortunately, not all submissions will be included in the exhibition, as our galleries have size limitations. Fees are non-refundable and used to support non-profit exhibition programming. Thank you for your submission!

Jul 1 – Aug 19, 2026 Continuing Pottery | 6-8:30 pm AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 6:00 pm · $535

Instructor: Samantha Radcliffe

Learn to center clay on the potters wheel, make mugs, bowls, pitchers, casseroles and much more. If you are a beginner or more advanced you will enjoy the clay experience. Acquire the skills of throwing, trimming, making handles, glazing and kiln firing.

$460 + $75 materials fee

8 weeks

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Jul 7 – Aug 18, 2026 Composition in Oils or Acrylics AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 5:30 pm · $400

Instructor: Susan Gray Bé

Elevate your painting by mastering the core principles of composition while you learn to balance color contrast, pattern, tone, and line. In this class you'll learn from the ground up beginning with a foundational charcoal drawing, before diving into paint to explore advanced techniques like underpainting, wet-into-wet blending, and the luminous effects of glazing.

Click here for the class materials list

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Jul 9 – Aug 27, 2026 Blacksmithing | 6-8:30 pm AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 6:00 pm · $75–$575

Instructor: Roff Harroff

In this class students will study the time-honored art of Blacksmithing to make three dimensional objects with iron. Using gas forges, anvils, hammer and tongs students will forge and form iron into sculptural and utilitarian pieces. This class is meant for students ages 16 and up.

8 weeks

$400 + $75 materials fee

Rated NR · 2:31

HADESTOWN: THE MUSICAL a live theater capture of the hit musical, which won eight Tony Awards including Best Musical and the Grammy Award for Best Musical Theater Album, featuring the five original principals of the Broadway company. The genre-defying new musical blends modern American folk music with New Orleans-inspired jazz to re-imagine a sweeping ancient tale. Following two intertwining love stories — that of young dreamers Orpheus and Eurydice, and that of King Hades and his wife Persephone – Hadestown invites audiences on a hell-raising journey to the underworld and back.

The genre-defying new musical blends modern American folk music with New Orleans-inspired jazz to reimagine a sweeping ancient tale. In Disney’s live-action adaptation of the beloved Oscar®-nominated animated adventure, Moana (Catherine Lagaʻaia) answers the Ocean’s call and, for the first time, voyages beyond the reef of her island of Motunui with the infamous demigod Maui (Dwayne Johnson) on an unforgettable journey to restore prosperity to her people. The film is directed by Emmy® and Tony Award® winner Thomas Kail (“Hamilton”); produced by Dwayne Johnson, Dany Garcia, Beau Flynn, Hiram Garcia and Lin-Manuel Miranda; and executive produced by Thomas Kail, Scott Sheldon, Charles Newirth and Auliʻi Cravalho, who voiced Moana in the animated features “Moana” and “Moana 2.” Audiences can experience the brilliant cinematic sights, sounds and songs of “Moana” in U.S. theaters on July 10, 2026.LessMore

Jul 24 Jul 25 Jul 26 Jul 27 Jul 28 Jul 29 Aug 6

Saturday, July 25, 2026

9:00am Celebrate 250America Kids Day! Moultonborough Public Library 935 Whittier Highway Moultonborough NH 03254

Join us on Saturday, July 25, from 9:00 am to 12:00 pm at the Moultonborough Historical Society for a festive morning celebrating America’s 250th birthday! This family-friendly event features a variety of hands-on activities, including painting wooden spinning tops, crafting paper tricorn hats, and trying your hand at fishing for apples. Step back in time at the historic schoolhouse to practice your alphabet on mini chalkboards, or visit the photo booth to dress up in historical clothing. While you're there, stop by the library station to sign up for Summer Reading and enjoy refreshments like popcorn and mini cupcakes on the museum porch. We can't wait to see you there for this historic community celebration!

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
Big WatchMoultonborough Public LibraryJosianne Fitzgerald, adultservices@moultonboroughlibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
10:00 am Berry Basket Workshop Enfield Shaker Museum

$65 – $75

Berry Basket Workshop

with Dona Nazarenko

Saturday, July 25, 2026

10:00 – 5:00pm

Join us in the Great Stone Dwelling dining room for a full day workshop!  Berry baskets were traditionally hand woven of oak or ash. They were designed to hold individual servings of berries. A Berry Basket has many uses such as for the gardener, or hobbyist and they also make a great storage container. Participants will use natural reed, paired with a wood handle, and can choose to add color accents if desired.  All skill levels welcome.  This square basket measures approximately 6 ½” x 6 ½” x 11 ½” high with handle.

Who is Doña Nazerenko?

Residing in Tunbridge, Vermont, Doña Nazarenko has been weaving since 1988 and teaching since 1994. While living along the New Jersey coast, Doña developed an interest in woodcrafts and related art forms that developed into her desire to take basket making classes in 1988.

Her exploration of a different art medium has since evolved into a flourishing talent. She now enjoys making traditional baskets using reed with wooden accents to more challenging baskets using birch bark from the area surrounding her current residence in rural Vermont.

The highlight of the early years at her craft was having a one-on-one critique with John McGuire at Peter’s Gallery in 1992. His confidence in her and his discretion continues to help her with her current work. Today she can be seen demonstrating basketry representing Vermont fiber artists at local and out-of-state events. Most days she can be found working in her studio at Country Spirit Baskets

Please bring your own lunch. Water and light refreshments will be provided.

Cost:

Museum Members – $65

Non-Members – $75

All proceeds go to the educational mission of Enfield Shaker Museum.

Coming from a distance? Stay at the historic Great Stone Dwelling. Check our room availability here.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
1:00pm Dublin Public Library 125th Celebration Dublin Public Library 1114 Main Street Dublin NH 03444

On July 25, 2026 we will be hosting a 125th celebration of DPL. A gathering around our towns peace pole will start our ceremony with words from local leader discussing the role of librarians and libraries as a source of illumination. An original musical composition commissioned for DPL's 125th will be performed by Zak Redler. A poetry reading by poet Robert Forbes, then local children will share what the library means to them. There will be a library themed scavenger hunt throughout town. We will then be inviting guests inside for a tour and talk of the library and it's history, as well as a history archive recording booth set up for locals to share stories. We will be making a booklist of the 125 most checked out books for DPL and there will be golden tickets hidden in books to find for prizes. Our staff will unveil a plaque in honor of the Library Directors. Special 125th merchandise that includes limited edition library cards, pins, tshirts, bookmarks and stickers will be available.

This event is supported in part by a Civic Life and Belonging Grant from New Hampshire Humanities.

Civic Life and Belonging GrantsDublin Public LibraryEmaline Bergeron director@dplnh.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
1:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Marlow Town Library 12 Church St. Marlow NH 03456

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Program to be followed by an Ice Cream Social sponsored by the Marlow Parks and Rec

Humanities to GoMary AdamsMarlow Town LibraryJeanne Kennedy, 603-852-2652
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
4:00 pm - 5:30 pm New York Theatre Workshop: Moonie Bentley Theater, Hopkins Center for the Arts

A solo show about lineage, love, motherhood and the grief that comes with it all.

Did you know that although you were carried in your mother's womb, the egg you came from was first formed in your grandmother's womb? Moonie didn't. This year, Moonie turns 30, and has decided to go on dates with 100 men this year. Today, she is going on her 88th. It is not because her Taiwanese mother forced her. Or because she is lonely. Or because she is afraid she won't know how to keep living when her mother dies. 

This event is free but tickets are required.

Get more info here.

This event is free but tickets are required.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Sunday, July 26, 2026

4 pm - 6 pm Her (DFS: Killer Robots!) Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

In the near future, a lonely writer develops an unlikely but emotionally resonant relationship with an operating system designed to meet his every need.

Written and directed by Oscar-nominated filmmaker Spike Jonze, Her centers on Theodore Twombly (Joaquin Phoenix), a complex, soulful man who lives in a near-future Los Angeles. 

Twombly, who is heartbroken by the recent end of a long relationship, makes a living writing touching, personal letters for others. He becomes intrigued, however, with a new "advanced operating system" that promises to be intuitive and unique in its own right. After initiating it, he meets "Samantha," a bright female voice (Scarlett Johansson) who proves insightful, sensitive and even funny. Soon, their needs and desires flourish together and their friendship deepens into an eventual love. 

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
4 pm - 9 pm New Energy Summer Summit Irving Institute

Now in its fifth year, the New Energy Summer Summit takes place July 26–29 at the Irving Institute. The Summit brings together early-career energy and climate scholars to increase their capacity to connect across disciplines and sectors to advance equitable and sustainable energy systems.

This year's cohort includes 31 participants, selected from more than 150 applications. Scholars come from all disciplines, including natural science, technology, humanities, art, health and medicine, social sciences, policy and law, and more. 

Over the course of four days, the cohort will engage in a dynamic program of lightning talks, breakout groups, panel discussions, workshops, and networking opportunities.

Learn more about the New Energy Summer Summit

Arthur L. Irving Institute for Energy and Society

4:00pm A Visit with Abraham Lincoln Whitney Community Center 16 Black Mountain Road Jackson NH 03846

Abraham Lincoln, portrayed by Steve Wood, begins this program by recounting his early life and ends with a reading of the "Gettysburg Address." Along the way he comments on the debates with Stephen Douglas, his run for the presidency, and the Civil War. 

Humanities to GoSteve WoodFriends of the Whitney Community CenterGreg Marsello, 603-307-0639 whitneycommunitycenter@gmail.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
4:00pm Who Was John Stark? Center Meeting House PO Box 50 Newbury NH 03255

While many are familiar with the state motto “live free or die,” few might know about the man, General John Stark, who uttered these words. In this presentation, George Morrison reveals the man – a ransomed captive, Ranger officer, road-builder, lumberman, husband and father, repeatedly called-on to serve but often clashing with authority -- whose phrase has become synonymous with our state. 

Humanities to GoGeorge MorrisonCenter Meeting HouseLea McBain, 603-763-5803

8 am - 9 am Monday Morning Meditation Rollins Chapel

Full details ↗
Jul 27 Aug 3 Aug 10 Aug 17 Aug 24 Aug 31 Sep 7 Sep 14 Sep 21 Sep 28 Oct 5 Oct 12 Oct 19
6:00pm US@250: Big Watch Film Screening & Discussion Dover Public Library 73 Locust St. Dover NH 03820

Join us to commemorate and reflect upon the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence with a film screening and discussion!

 

Together, we’ll watch a short excerpt from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION, a new film directed by Ken Burns, Sarah Botstein and David Schmidt, followed by a community discussion. The featured segment from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION,“The Spirit of Service,” explores service as a core American value, from the nation’s founding through to the present day – examining volunteerism, civic participation, public leadership, and more. Expert facilitators will guide thoughtful conversations about “remembering together,” considering questions such as:

    • What moments from our past define who we are and point to a shared future?
    • What are the key historical moments in your town’s or community’s history?
    • How does focusing on these moments shape your sense of your community’s future?


Interested in watching the entire film series? Learn more here: The American Revolution | All Episodes Now Streaming | Ken Burns | PBS


Big Watch programs are made possible by a partnership between New Hampshire Humanities and New Hampshire PBS, with support from Cogswell Benevolent Trust. 

Corporate funding for THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was provided by Bank of America. Major funding was provided by The Better Angels Society and its members Jeannie and Jonathan Lavine with the Crimson Lion Foundation; and the Blavatnik Family Foundation. Major funding was also provided by David M. Rubenstein; The Robert D. and Patricia E. Kern Family Foundation; Lilly Endowment Inc.; and the 
following Better Angels Society members: Eric and Wendy Schmidt; Stephen A. Schwarzman; and Kenneth C. Griffin with Griffin Catalyst. Additional support for THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was provided by: The Arthur Vining Davis Foundations; The Pew Charitable Trusts; Gilbert S. Omenn and Martha A. Darling; Park Foundation; and the following Better Angels Society members: Gilchrist and Amy Berg; 
Perry and Donna Golkin; The Michelson Foundation; Jacqueline B. Mars; Kissick Family Foundation; Diane and Hal Brierley; John H. N. Fisher and Jennifer Caldwell; John and Catherine Debs; The Fullerton Family Charitable Fund; Philip I. Kent; Gail Elden; Deborah and Jon Dawson; David and Susan Kreisman; The McCloskey Family Charitable Trust; Becky and Jim Morgan; Carol and Ned Spieker; Mark A. Tracy; 
and Paul and Shelley Whyte. THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was made possible, in part, with support from the Corporation for Public Broadcasting.

By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community-driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.

 

Register here.

Free parking is available in the lot behind the library. Elevator access to the top floor Community Room.
Come and visit the newly renovated library!

Add to Google Calendar

Tuesday, July 28, 2026

All day Century of Civic Sound Exhibit Baker Library, Reiss Hall

All dates: Jul 28, Aug 4, Aug 11, Aug 18, Aug 25, Sep 1, Sep 8, Sep 15, Sep 22, Sep 29, Oct 6

May 19 through October 09, 2026
Baker-Berry Library, Reiss Hall
Curated by Memory Apata, Research & Learning Librarian for Music & Performing Arts
Designed by Max Seidman, Exhibits and Graphic Arts Designer

 

Dartmouth's Soundscape

Generations of community members have shaped Dartmouth's sonic identity through the Baker Bells: from the machinist who hacked player pianos to control the clock tower and the professors' wives who hand-punched hundreds of music rolls, to the student composers writing original works for the tower today. Explore the exhibit and consider: how does sound shape your sense of place? What should Dartmouth sound like and who should decide?

Library

Jul 28 Aug 4 Aug 11 Aug 18 Aug 25 Sep 1 Sep 8 Sep 15 Sep 22 Sep 29 Oct 6
All day Motivational Interviewing 2 Day Training To be determined

All dates: Jul 28, Jul 30

https://forms.gle/5DKZPUcn6VV3pGmV9 (Registration)

Summer 2026 Motivational Interviewing Training—Please register by July 15.

The Dialogue Project: Motivational Interviewing Two-Day Workshop

Tuesday, July 28 and Thursday, July 30
9 a.m.–4:30 p.m. (morning refreshments available at 8:30 a.m.)

 

Location: on campus at Dartmouth College, exact location to be determined

The Student Wellness Center, with support from the Dialogue Project, offers this intensive, interactive (and fun!) two-day workshop aimed at building basic proficiency in Motivational Interviewing (MI). MI is a collaborative communication style for supporting individuals through growth and change.

 

MI trainings are named in Dartmouth's Commitment to Care strategic plan as “effective skill-building trainings that support high-quality mentoring, advising, and teaching.” 

This evidence-based approach has broad application for working with students (and others) and can be an instrumental tool for advisors, coaches, facilitators, and educators. The training is free and open to the entire Dartmouth community. Morning refreshments, snacks, and lunch will be provided.

 

Facilitated by Caitlin Barthelmes, member of the MI Network of Trainers and LB White of the Student Wellness Center. 

 *Please attend both days. We offer this training twice a year. If you can't commit to the full training this time, please plan to attend the event when it is offered in December 2026.

 

Student Wellness Center

12 pm - 1 pm Beat the Heat: Summer Safety Event audience: Faculty, Postdoc, Staff Baker 234

Summer's here, and so are we — with everything you need to stay cool, hydrated, and protected all season long. Stop by for a quick chat with our health coach about sun safety and hydration, then grab your free outdoor necessities kit and a reusable water bottle, enjoy a chilled Spindrift seltzer, enter a raffle for a chance to win a disc golf set, and more!

No RSVP needed — just drop by anytime between 12 - 1 PM.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
2 pm - 3 pm Physics and Astronomy Quantum Nano Seminar - Simon Martin, UCSD academic Wilder 202 and Zoom

Abstract: Quantum phase transitions (QPTs) are zero-temperature phase transitions driven by quantum fluctuations and accessed by tuning a non-thermal parameter. In this talk, I will discuss two types of unconventional QPTs, primarily from a field-theoretic perspective. First, I will present a model consisting of a lattice of single-impurity multichannel Kondo dots, for which analytical and numerical results reveal a “dimensionality-changing” quantum phase transition between dynamically decoupled zero-dimensional non-Fermi liquids and a one-dimensional valence-bond-solid phase. I will then turn to QPTs involving symmetric mass generation (SMG), a new mechanism for gapping fermions beyond the conventional Landau-Ginzburg paradigm. I will discuss a two-dimensional lattice model in which previous numerical studies established SMG, and for which we have developed a perturbative framework to identify and characterize the universal properties of the associated quantum critical point.

Hosted by Assistant Professor Rufus Boyack

Zoom Meeting Link: https://dartmouth.zoom.us/j/98435866316 

Physics & Astronomy Department

Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Maxfield Public Library 8 NH-129 Loudon NH 03307

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Humanities to GoMary AdamsMaxfield Public LibraryJess Charland, jcharland@maxfieldlibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm Film Screening with Rich Fedorchak South Studio | AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Free

Part of an occasional art film screening hosted by AVA member Rick Fedorchak. Films selected for the July 28 include short experimental films by Rich and a film by the musician and composer Reagan Padula who will perform a live piece of music to accompany her film.

Rich Fedorchak is a retired RN who has had an art practice in collage, assemblage and experimental film since the mid-1970's. He has been a longtime AVA exhibiting artist and volunteer. His films have been selected for screening at numerous film festivals devoted to experimental film in the US, Canada, and Italy.

Reagan Padula is a composer, instrumentalist and sound organizer whose ears are moving as microphone and speaker. Right now she is investigating sympathetic resonance sonic- collage and the disruption of memory-time, body-space, self-other. She feels fulfillment from creating and studying queer art spaces in the underground and how they heal communities.For the past 2 years she has been the Post-Baccalaureate Intern and teaching assistant in the masters program in Sonic Practice at Dartmouth College.

Wednesday, July 29, 2026

All day 26F DPCS Design Your Own (DYO) Internship Funding Deadline audience: Faculty, Public, Staff, Students-Undergraduate Online Application

Design your own in-person internship experience. Work to support the mission of a remarkable nonprofit, make an impact on an under-resourced community, advance yourself professionally, and receive first-rate mentorship from an alumni mentor. Pick an issue to engage more deeply or a skill you'd like to master and design your internship around that aspiration.

For over 25 years Dartmouth Partners in Community Service (DPCS) has provided funding and mentorship for students wishing to pursue unpaid internships with domestic nonprofits serving under-resourced communities. 

Advising is highly recommended before submitting an application. Advising is highly recommended before submitting an application. Schedule an advising appointment - meet with our staff or student director of internships to build an effective project plan. 

Faculty, Public, Staff, Students-UndergraduateDartmouth Center for Social Impact

9 am - 2 pm Dartmouth Official Gear Store Class of 1953 Commons 104 Paganucci Lounge

This new agreement is designed to simplify the purchasing process, ensure brand consistency, and provide competitive pricing for departments and programs across campus.

To introduce this new service and give you the opportunity to see the quality and range of products firsthand, we invite you to join us at a Vendor Fair on:

Wednesday, July 29

Paganucci Lounge

10 am - 2 pm

At the fair, you will have the chance to:

• Meet the vendor team and learn about available services

• Browse and handle product samples

• Ask questions about ordering, customization, and turnaround times

• Learn how to get started under the new contract

Whether you regularly purchase promotional items, manage apparel orders, or play a role in designing branded merchandise for your department or organization, we encourage you to attend.

More details about the contract and the new ordering process will be shared at the event and in follow-up communications.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
10:00am Songs of Emigration: Storytelling Through Traditional Irish Music Blaisdell Memorial Library 129 Stage Road Nottingham NH 03290

Through traditional music Jordan Tirrell-Wysocki relays some of the adventures, misadventures, and emotions experienced by Irish emigrants. The focus is on songs about leaving Ireland, sometimes focusing on the reasons for leaving (a man who is driven from his land by English persecution), sometimes revealing what happened upon arrival (an immigrant drafted into the Union army during the Civil War), and sometimes exploring the universal feeling of homesickness of a stranger in a strange land (a factory worker in London missing his home in County Clare). The presenter discusses the historical context of these songs, interspersing their stories with tunes from Ireland that made their way into New England's musical repertoire, played on his fiddle or guitar. 

Humanities to GoJordan Tirrell-WysockiBlaisdell Memorial LibraryDan Bunker, 603-679-8484

12:00 pm - 12:45 pm Wednesday Virtual Mindfulness Practice Group audience: Alumni, Faculty, Postdoc, Staff, Students-Graduate, Students-Undergraduate Zoom

All dates: Jul 29, Aug 5, Aug 12, Aug 19, Aug 26, Sep 2, Sep 9, Sep 16, Sep 23, Sep 30, Oct 7, Oct 14, Oct 21

Join our weekly virtual mindfulness practice group to deepen your mindfulness and meditation practice while connecting with others in a supportive space. Each session includes a 20–25 minute guided sit followed by a brief discussion about our experiences in meditation and mindfulness practice. An optional reading will be offered each week to help guide or enrich the conversation, though completing it is not required. Open to the entire Dartmouth community; all levels of experience are welcome. Join as often as you can!

Register to receive reminders and links to the optional readings.

Sponsored by: Wellness at Dartmouth, Tucker Center, and the Student Wellness Center.

Full details ↗
Jul 29 Aug 5 Aug 12 Aug 19 Aug 26 Sep 2 Sep 9 Sep 16 Sep 23 Sep 30 Oct 7 Oct 14 Oct 21
12:30 pm - 1:30 pm Publishing Talk: Jason Roberts & Peter Orner Literary Arts Bridge (7 Lebanon St. Suite 105)

Jason Roberts is an American write of nonfiction and fiction. His most recent book, Every Living Thing, was awarded the 2025 Pulitzer Prize for biography, as well as the PEN America/E.O. Wilson Library Science Writing award. His previous book, A Sense of the World, was a national bestseller, a finalist for the National Book Critics circle Award, and longlisted for the international Guardian First Book Award. He lives in Oakland and Montreal.

Born in Chicago, Peter Orner is the author of eight acclaimed books, including The Gossip Columnist's Daughter; Maggie Brown & Others; Love and Shame and Love; Esther Stories, finalist for the PEN/Hemingway Award; and Am I Alone Here?, a finalist for the National Book Critics Circle Award. His work has appeared in The New Yorker, the Paris Review, The Believer, and The Best American Short Stories, and received four Pushcart Prizes. A former Guggenheim fellow and recipient of the Rome Prize from the American Academy of Arts and Letters, Orner is professor of Creative Writing at Dartmouth College. He lives with his family in Vermont, where he's also a volunteer firefighter.

Literary Arts Bridge

2:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Bedford Public Library 3 Meetinghouse Road Bedford NH 03110

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

Please join us for a reading with Jason Roberts on Wednesday, July 29 at 4:30 pm in the air-conditioned Haldeman 041, as part of the Cleopatra Mathis Poetry & Prose Reading Series. Jason Roberts is a writer of nonfiction and fiction. His most recent book, Every Living Thing, was awarded the 2025 Pulitzer Prize for biography, as well as the PEN America/E.O. Wilson Literary Science Writing award. His previous book, A Sense of the World, was a national bestseller, a finalist for the National Book Critics Circle Award, and longlisted for the international Guardian First Book Award. He lives in Oakland and Montreal. The reading will be followed by a Q&A and book signing. Books will be for sale at the event with the Norwich Bookstore. For access needs or with questions, please email english.department@dartmouth.edu.

6:00pm US@250: Big Watch Film Screening & Discussion Auburn Historical Association 102 Hooksett Rd. Auburn NH 03032

Join us to commemorate and reflect upon the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence with a film screening and discussion!

 

Together, we’ll watch a short excerpt from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION, a new film directed by Ken Burns, Sarah Botstein and David Schmidt, followed by a community discussion. The featured segment from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION,“The Spirit of Service,” explores service as a core American value, from the nation’s founding through to the present day – examining volunteerism, civic participation, public leadership, and more. Expert facilitators will guide thoughtful conversations about “remembering together,” considering questions such as:

    • What moments from our past define who we are and point to a shared future?
    • What are the key historical moments in your town’s or community’s history?
    • How does focusing on these moments shape your sense of your community’s future?


Interested in watching the entire film series? Learn more here: The American Revolution | All Episodes Now Streaming | Ken Burns | PBS


Big Watch programs are made possible by a partnership between New Hampshire Humanities and New Hampshire PBS, with support from Cogswell Benevolent Trust.

Kent; Gail Elden; Deborah and Jon Dawson; David and Susan Kreisman; The McCloskey Family Charitable Trust; Becky and Jim Morgan; Carol and Ned Spieker; Mark A. Tracy;

6:00 pm Free Outdoor Concert – Never Too Late Enfield Shaker Museum

2026 Free Outdoor Concert Series

Never Too Late

Wednesday, July 29, 2026

Live music starts at 7 pm. Vending begins at 6 pm.

Join us outside the historic Stone Machine Shop for a FREE concert with Never Too Late!

Never Too Late creates Americana and classic folk music performances with rich vocal harmonies accompanied by lively instrumentation.  Whether the performance is an outdoor festival or intimate coffeehouse, Never Too Late fully engages the audience for a fun and memorable experience.  Band members include:

Singer-songwriter Hilde Ojibway creates songs for the band that reflect life’s light and textures; Multi-talented Adam Sorscher stirs with his mandolin, guitar, banjo and vocal harmonies; Outstanding bassist Eric Bronstein marries folk and jazz traditions to ground the band’s sound; and Stunning vocalist Bethany Nafzinger soars with solo performances and heavenly harmonies.

It’s NEVER TOO LATE to share the joy of music!

Bring your chairs and blankets and enjoy this free outdoor concert at the Enfield Shaker Museum Stone Machine Shop.

Beer, wine, soda, and snacks will be available to purchase during the concert. (No private alcohol allowed.)

Faz’s Food Truck will be on site.

Live music will start at 7 pm. Vending will begin at 6 pm.

Rain or shine (we will move inside in case of rain.) Plenty of free parking.

THANK YOU TO OUR SPONSORS:

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:30 pm - 7:30 pm LECTURE Howe Library and on Zoom

“Excavations and Exhibitions: Digging into Archeology and the Art Museum”

Hood Museum of Art and Howe Library are collaborating on this year’s national summer reading theme, “Unearth a Story,” with particular reference to paleontology and archeology. The Howe has chosen to read Otherlands: A Journey Through Earth’s Extinct Worlds, by Thomas Halliday. In connection with this theme, Hood Curator of Collections Ashley Offill will explore the instrumental role of archeology in forming many early museum collections and the legacies and lessons of archeological objects in the modern museum with reference to the ongoing exhibitions Stone, Sand, and Clay: Connecting Cultures in the Ancient Mediterranean and From Mastodon to Mosaic: Building an Academic Art Collection in America. This hybrid program is free and open to all. To attend via Zoom, please register here. 

Photographs may be taken at this event for promotional or educational use. By attending, you consent to be photographed and/or video recorded. Please contact us if you have any questions or concerns.

Hood Museum of Art
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 8:30 pm Exhibition on Screen: Turner & Constable Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

This new documentary charts the legacy and rivalry of two of Britain's greatest painters alongside the groundbreaking Tate exhibition.

Born within a year of each other—and celebrating their own 250th along with America—J.M.W. Turner and John Constable both used landscape painting to reflect the changing world around them. Turner's blazing sunsets and sublime scenes from his travels and Constable's idealised depictions of beloved places from home whipped the public of the time into a frenzy of enthusiasm. Constable represents the very best of the old school of realism and pastoral nostalgia; Turner, an exciting new way of depicting emotion and dreamlike impressions. Critics compared their starkly different styles to a clash of 'fire and water.' 

Tate Britain brought these two greats together for a groundbreaking exhibition in London in 2026, and this documentary brings that remarkable showcase of "Rivals & Originals" to the big screen. Discover unexpected sides to both artists with intimate views of sketchbooks and personal items, as leading experts offer insights gleaned from seeing these greats side-by-side, as they so often were in life.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts

7:00pm US@250: You Never Forget Your First Walpole Town Library 48 Main Street Walpole NH 03608

A book discussion of You Never Forget Your First by Alexis Coe. This book takes a fresh look at George Washington, moving beyond the heroic stories many of us learned in school to show him as a real, complicated person. Whether you love history or are just curious to learn something new, this conversation will be welcoming, thoughtful, and easy to enjoy.

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Spider-Man: Brand New Day Nugget Theater, Hanover

All dates: Jul 29, Jul 30

Wed Jul 29Closed
Thu Jul 3012:50 · 3:40 · 6:40

Rated PG-13 · 2:35

Tickets on Sale For Thurs, July 30 – Thurs, August 6

It’s a BRAND NEW DAY for Peter Parker. Fighting crime full-time as Spider-Man in a world that doesn’t remember him — and the pressure of seeing his old friends move on without him — sparks a change in Peter he may not have the power to control. But that transformation might also be the only thing that can stop a shocking new threat to the city and those he loves – a  powerful villain no one can even see. The world may have forgotten Peter Parker, but he hasn’t forgotten them.

Full details ↗Trailer ▶
Jul 29 Jul 30

Thursday, July 30, 2026

8:00 am - 8:45 am Thursday Morning Meditation Rollins Chapel

All dates: Jul 30, Aug 6, Aug 13, Aug 20, Aug 27

Each week, we will sit for 25 minutes of guided meditation followed by discussion. Feel free to leave at 8:30 after the meditation or stay for the discussion. Open to students, staff, faculty and community members. No experience needed.

Sponsors: Mindfullness @ Dartmouth, Student Wellness Center, Tucker Center, & Wellness @ Dartmouth.

Questions? Contact the Tucker Center at: tucker.center@dartmouth.edu. 

 

Mindful Dartmouth, Mindful Dartmouth, Mindful Dartmouth, Student Wellness Center, Student Wellness Center, Student Wellness Center, Tucker Center, Wellness at Dartmouth, Wellness at Dartmouth, Wellness at Dartmouth
Full details ↗
Jul 30 Aug 6 Aug 13 Aug 20 Aug 27
5:30pm Last of the Granite State Rattlesnakes Chocorua Public Library 25 Deer Hill Road Tamworth NH 03817

Rattlesnakes were so common in colonial-era New Hampshire that dozens of places were named for them, ranging from the Massachusetts border to the White Mountains. They were so abundant some settlers could pay their property taxes with the bounties offered for killing them. Today, however, Crotalus horridus, the timber rattlesnake, is the state’s rarest native animal—only a handful are known to survive. State law protects them now, but that may not be enough to save them. Wildlife biologists have predicted this tiny remnant will die out without extraordinary intervention.

How did this feared and fascinating animal come to the very brink of extirpation in our state? This illustrated presentation maps how we got here through the social, political, and natural history of New Hampshire’s rattlesnakes. Historian and former newspaper reporter Dan Billin presents vivid, little-known stories and images from our centuries-long war on our rattlesnakes. Topics include who’s been bitten; the medicinal use of rattlesnake oil by the Shakers and others; the exploits of the “Rattlesnake King of New Hampshire”; the twentieth-century rattlesnake panic in the legislature; and the mysterious recent appearance of rattlesnakes with Pennsylvania genes. This program reviews what is and isn’t being done today to conserve our rattlesnakes, and the many obstacles to their survival.

Humanities to GoDan BillinChocorua Public LibraryShauna Peterson, 603-323-8610
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm What's Your Story? Gay Kimball Library 10 S. Main St Troy NH 03465

This program is a storytelling presentation and workshop that highlights how stories connect us. Presented by Author Veronica Francis, in conjunction with librarian and narrative therapist Erin George, this program will demonstrate connections and commonalities neighbors might not realize they have, despite living nearby for years.

Veronica is a New Hampshire storyteller, keynote speaker, and technology coach who helps people navigate change with clarity, confidence, and a sense of humor. Known for her warm presence and relatable storytelling, Veronica blends real-life experience with practical insight - reminding audiences that adaptability matters more than perfection, especially when the plan falls apart.

This program is free and open to the public, will include refreshments, and will begin promptly at 6 pm. You do not have to be a Troy resident or Gay-Kimball Library patron to participate.

This event is made possible in part by a Civic Life and Belonging Grant from New Hampshire Humanities.

Civic Life and Belonging GrantsGay Kimball LibraryErin George troylibrarynh@gmail.com

Add to Google Calendar
6:00 pm Herbal Remedy Making with Maria Noël Groves Chosen Vale Performance Center, Enfield

$70 – $75

Herbal Remedy Making with Maria Noël Groves

Thursday, July 30, 6-8 pm 

In Person at Enfield Shaker Museum, 447 NH Route 4A, Enfield, NH

Join us for Herbal remedy Making with Maria Noel Groves. Your garden is likely at its peak right now and you might be wondering: What can I do with all those wonderful culinary and medicinal plants to enjoy them all winter long? In this remedy demo class, Maria will share her favorite home apothecary techniques to dry herbs and make herbal extracts with fresh or dry plants including alcohol-based tinctures, vinegar’s, glycerites, honey, and oxymel. She’ll demonstrate various techniques, provide recipes, share storage tips, and you’ll have the opportunity to make an extract jar to bring home.
https://wintergreenbotanicals.com/
Maria Noël Groves, RH(AHG), clinical herbalist, runs Wintergreen Botanicals, nestled in the pine forests of New Hampshire. Her business is devoted to education and empowerment via classes, health consultations, and writing with the foundational belief that good health grows in nature. She is the author of the award-winning, best-selling Body into Balance: An Herbal Guide to Holistic Self Care, Grow Your Own Herbal Remedies, Herbal Remedies for Sleep, Herbal Gut Health, and an additional forthcoming book. Learn more about Maria and herbs at WintergreenBotanicals.com.
*All proceeds go to the educational mission of Enfield Shaker Museum
Coming from a distance? Stay in our historic Great Stone Dwelling. Check our room availability here.

Add to Google Calendar
6:00 pm Taboo Tour – July 31st Enfield Shaker Museum

$45 – $50

Taboo Tour

at the Enfield Shaker Museum

Step beyond the traditional history tour and explore the darker, more mysterious side of Shaker life. The Enfield Shaker Museum presents a special series of three Taboo Tours, each examining fascinating and controversial topics from Shaker history, including scandal, murder, spiritualism, the afterlife, and the enduring question: Were the Shakers a cult?

Each evening features a unique theme, an interactive hands-on activity, and an opportunity to uncover stories rarely told on standard tours. Guests will also enjoy one complimentary cocktail, beer, or glass of wine with their ticket, along with light refreshments.

Tour Dates:

  • July 31 – 6:00 – 8:00 pm
  • August 21 – 6:00 – 8:00 pm
  • October 30 – 6:00 – 8:00 pm

Cost:

Museum Members – $45 per person

Non-Memvers – $50 per person
Capacity: Limited to 20 participants per tour

Join us for an unforgettable evening of history, mystery, and conversation as we uncover the secrets, beliefs, and controversies hidden within the Enfield Shaker community.

7:00 pm - 8:45 pm Summer Film: Sundance Shorts Tour Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

A showcase of seven specially curated shorts from the 2026 Sundance Film Festival, the preeminent launchpad for young and new filmmaking talent.

Audiences who crave a bit of that Sundance Film Festival creativity can enjoy a mix of fiction, documentary and animated shorts that are funny, sad, inspiring and full of strong characters. Driven by innovation and experimentation, the Short Film Program seeks out filmmaking's most original voices from around the world. Past alumni of the shorts program include Andrea Arnold, Lake Bell, Damien Chazelle, Jay and Mark Duplass, Debra Granik, Todd Haynes, Dee Rees, Taika Waititi and many others. 

Get more info and tickets here.

Jul 30 Jul 31 Aug 1 Aug 2 Aug 3 Aug 4 Aug 5

Friday, July 31, 2026

11:00 am Shaker Herb Garden Tour Enfield Shaker Museum Garden

All dates: Jul 31, Aug 7, Aug 14, Aug 21, Aug 28, Sep 4, Sep 11, Sep 18, Sep 25, Oct 2, Oct 9, Oct 16

Weekly Shaker Herb Garden Tour

with Herbalist Happy Griffiths

Fridays at 11 am, meet by the rose bushes in the garden

Join renowned herbalist and founder of the Enfield Shaker Museum Herb Garden, Happy Griffiths, for a guided tour through one of the Museum’s most beloved living exhibits.

Discover the fascinating history of the Shakers’ herb-growing traditions as you explore beds of culinary, medicinal, aromatic, and dye plants. Along the way, Happy will share stories of how these herbs were cultivated and used by the Enfield Shakers, as well as their practical uses in today’s gardens and homes.

With decades of experience as an herbalist, educator, and the visionary behind the Museum’s herb garden, Happy brings each tour to life with engaging insights and a deep appreciation for the connections between people, plants, and history. The Museum’s herb garden features many of the same plants that were grown by the Shakers as part of their thriving herb and medicine enterprise.

Fridays at 11:00 a.m.

Tours are free with Museum admission and are open to visitors of all ages. Whether you’re an avid gardener, curious about herbal traditions, or simply looking for a beautiful way to experience the Museum grounds, this tour offers a unique opportunity to explore the living legacy of the Enfield Shakers.

What to Expect

Tours last approximately one hour and take place rain or shine, except in the case of thunder or lightning. Much of the tour is outdoors in full sun, so we recommend dressing for the weather, wearing comfortable walking shoes, and bringing water, sunscreen, or rain gear as needed.

The herb garden features maintained paths, but visitors should expect some uneven terrain. Benches are available in both sunny and shaded areas throughout the garden for those who would like to sit during the tour.

Visitors are encouraged to engage all of their senses. You’ll have opportunities to see, smell, and ~when invited by your guide~ even taste selected herbs. To help preserve the garden and support the Museum’s educational mission, we ask that visitors do not pick plants or harvest herbs during the tour.

Add to Google Calendar
5:00 pm Converse with the Crossroads of the Winds Main Galleries (First Floor), Lebanon

Free

Poet and painter Alice Paalen Rahon (1904–1987) wrote, “I’ve been living in a map on the wall. I think I am at the crossroads of the wind. I converse with it. Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind will bring together a small cohort of artists selected through a competitive open call. How does change (good, bad, or ugly) influence new directions within our studio practice and process?

The exhibition concept supports artists whose practices thrive within ambiguity, inquiry, and material exploration. Thematically, Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind centers on ideas that engages liminal states of being through uncertainty, motion, and environmental or political thresholds. Artists are encouraged to present works that engage with change, approached from various angles of discovery—be it personal perspective, socio-political opinion, cultural memory, or bold and experimental narrative techniques.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 9:15 pm The Matrix (DFS: Killer Robots!) Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Keanu Reeves learns kung fu to take on Hugo Weaving and his digital henchmen in this 1999 cyber thriller about the threats and possibilities of an increasingly virtual world.

In retrospect, this 1999 action thriller seems terrifyingly prescient, with its vision of a future where humankind is enslaved by an elaborate digital façade, while a malevolent cyber-intelligence dominates the "real" world. Whoa.

Hacker Neo (Keanu Reeves) takes the red pill and reluctantly joins rebels Morpheus (Laurence Fishburne) and Trinity (Carrie Anne Moss) in their struggle against "the Matrix." But learning kung fu and how to dodge bullets might not be enough against the digital henchmen (Hugo Weaving) determined to protect the system—it might require a completely new understanding of what is "real." 

 Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Jul 31 – Sep 12, 2026 Opening Reception: Friday, July 31, 5-7 PM Main Gallery- AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 10:00 am · Free

AVA Gallery and Art Center is honored to present four new exhibitions in the main galleries, July 31 – September 12, Opening Reception, Friday, July 31, 5-7 PM

Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind Brings Together Six Artists Exploring Resilience, Transformation, and the Natural World. Cheryl Betz, Thia Daniels, Marisa Green, Patty Hudak, Carla Kimball, and Anna Prikazchikova — are featured in Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind, a group exhibition examining what it means to remain present and rooted at the intersection of forces beyond our control. The wind does not ask permission before it changes direction. It arrives, shifts the landscape, and moves on, leaving behind evidence of its passing in bent branches, scattered seeds, and the long memory of those who stood in its path. The exhibition spans painting, installation, printmaking, video, and papercut, with each artist approaching themes of cycles, loss, and transformation through a distinct visual language.

If These Feet Could Fly, a collection of paintings by Natasha Wein made entirely with her feet — an act of adaptation, resistance, and devotion born from the experience of living inside a body in decline. In 2019, Natasha Wein was diagnosed with Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome, a connective tissue disorder whose multi-system effects and spine injuries made her usual painting practice impossible. She taped paper to the floor and used her feet as brushes. The work continued. Then in 2024, she was struck by a car as a pedestrian, sustaining craniocervical instability and a traumatic brain injury. She now lives with intractable migraine, significantly diminished cognitive capacity, and hours each day spent resting in a dark, quiet room. If These Feet Could Fly emerges from that darkness — and insists on beauty anyway.

Steve Arcone and Talia Roberts: a dual exhibition pairing two artists whose practices, though rooted in different mediums and geographies, converge in a shared devotion to the slow, patient observation of the natural world. Steve Arcone | Icescapes in the Abstract: Images from New Hampshire Ponds From 2021 to 2026, Arcone photographed the fleeting architectures produced by freeze-thaw cycles on small New Hampshire ponds — star-like crystal growths radiating from host twigs, ladder-like mosaics born of crystallographic twinning, melt pools coexisting with intact ice, and complex fractures — all at intimate scales of 8 to 40 centimeters. The macro lens collapses depth and estranges the familiar, merging scientific process with abstract beauty. Organic debris offers occasional clues to context, but the images resist easy identification. Titles drawn from classical and jazz compositions offer starting points; viewers are encouraged to linger and offer what they see. Talia Roberts | Earth Memories, Moon Visions— Roberts collected rock samples from Vermont's Green Mountains, grinding them by hand into pigments and mulling each with walnut oil to produce non-synthetic oil paint whose palette carries the particular gravity of place. The resulting paintings, presented in pairs, turn on a recurring circular motif — an image of the moon — to explore duality: earth and sky, light and dark, memory and vision, the geological and the cosmic. By foregrounding materiality and process, Roberts asks viewers to slow down, to resist visual saturation, and to attend to the quiet beauty held in the natural world. Arcone and Roberts arrive at their subjects by different means, together, these exhibitions become a meditation on scale and looking — bridging the microscopic and the geological, the earthly and the infinite.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Jul 31 – Sep 12, 2026 Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind Main Gallery- AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 10:00 am · Free

Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind

Brings Together Six Artists Exploring Resilience, Transformation, and the Natural World

July 31 – September 12 | Clifford B. West and Rebecca Lawrence Gallery

Six artists — Cheryl Betz, Thia Daniels, Marisa Green, Patty Hudak, Carla Kimball, and Anna Prikazchikova — are featured in Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind, a group exhibition examining what it means to remain present and rooted at the intersection of forces beyond our control.

The exhibition spans painting, installation, printmaking, video, and papercut, with each artist approaching themes of cycles, loss, and transformation through a distinct visual language. Together, the six artists propose that making something beautiful in difficult times is not escape but resistance, but a turning toward rather than away. Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind is an invitation to stand at that intersection and listen.

The exhibition opens with a public reception on Friday, July 31, from 5 to 7 p.m. at the Clifford B. West and Rebecca Lawrence Gallery and runs through August 29.

Image credit: Patty Hudak, There Through the Broken Branches, Go, Acrylic on Voile (detail)

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Saturday, August 1, 2026

10:00 am Cyanotypes at Sweetland Farm | 10 am-3 pm Sweetland Farm, Norwich

$25–$180

Instructor: Larissa Burak

This summer join Larissa Burak at Sweetland Farm to explore the historical photographic process of the cyanotype. In this hands-on workshop, students will learn how to mix two-part, iron-based cyanotype chemistry; practice coating techniques; experiment with composition using fresh botanicals from the gardens at Sweetland Farm; gauge exposure times using available sunlight; and develop and oxidize prints.

Students are encouraged to bring additional two-dimensional objects they wish to use in their printing process, including lace, stencils, dried botanicals, and more.

$95+$25 materials fee

Rain Date: Saturday July 18th

12:00 pm Shaker Herb Garden Tour Enfield Shaker Museum Garden

All dates: Aug 1, Aug 8, Aug 15, Aug 22, Aug 29, Sep 5, Sep 12, Sep 19, Sep 26, Oct 3, Oct 10, Oct 17

Weekly Shaker Herb Garden Tour

with Museum Interpreter Lisa Torres

Saturdays at Noon, meet by the Great Stone Dwelling, outside the Gift Shop Entrance

Join Museum Interpreter Lisa Torres for a guided tour through the Enfield Shaker Museum Herb Garden, one of the Museum’s most beloved living exhibits.

Discover the fascinating history of the Shakers’ herb-growing traditions as you explore beds of culinary, medicinal, aromatic, and dye plants. Along the way, Lisa will share stories of how these herbs were cultivated and used by the Enfield Shakers, while exploring the ecology, botany, and practical uses of the plants growing in the garden today.

A lifelong science educator with a background in environmental chemistry and ecology, Lisa brings curiosity, enthusiasm, and a love of learning to every tour. After a 31-year career teaching science at Lebanon High School, she joined the Museum as an Interpreter and educator, where she enjoys helping visitors discover the rich connections between plants, history, and the Shaker way of life.

The Museum’s herb garden features many of the same plants that were grown by the Shakers as part of their thriving herb and medicine enterprise.

Saturdays at Noon.

Tours are free with Museum admission and are open to visitors of all ages. Whether you’re an avid gardener, curious about herbal traditions, or simply looking for a beautiful way to experience the Museum grounds, this tour offers a unique opportunity to explore the living legacy of the Enfield Shakers.

What to Expect

Tours last approximately one hour and take place rain or shine, except in the case of thunder or lightning. Much of the tour is outdoors in full sun, so we recommend dressing for the weather, wearing comfortable walking shoes, and bringing water, sunscreen, or rain gear as needed.

The herb garden features maintained paths, but visitors should expect some uneven terrain. Benches are available in both sunny and shaded areas throughout the garden for those who would like to sit during the tour.

Visitors are encouraged to engage all of their senses. You’ll have opportunities to see, smell, and ~when invited by your guide~ even taste selected herbs. To help preserve the garden and support the Museum’s educational mission, we ask that visitors do not pick plants or harvest herbs during the tour.

1:00 pm Charcoal Portraits AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

All dates: Aug 1, Aug 8

$0–$112.50

Instructor: Susan Gray Bé

See light as contrasting shape while adding structure and detail with line, tone, and color. Gesture drawing, brief then longer poses will lead to a finished Portrait in Charcoal.

Materials needed:

  • One box of extra soft vine charcoal
  • One pad of smooth Bristol paper
  • Two double pointed french blending stumps
  • Masking tape
  • Two medium size kneaded erasers

Aug 1 Aug 8
1:00 pm Eco-Printing: Surface Design with Plants at Sweetland Farm | 1-4 pm Sweetland Farm, Norwich

$25–$125

Instructor: Katie Spencer

Eco-printing, flower hammering, and bundle dyeing are a fun way to transfer color from plants to textiles. In this class, we'll explore both intentional surface designs with plants and also embrace free-form, expressive designs with plants. Sweetland Farm is a great location for an eco-printing class, as they are generously allowing us access to some fresh blooms as well as their compost bins, and both have much to offer the natural dyer looking to make uniquely patterned fabric. Participants will leave the day with a patterned cotton bandana of their own creation & the knowledge to make eco-prints at home.

$60+$25 materials fee

Rain Date: Sunday, August 2

4:00 pm - 5:30 pm New York Theatre Workshop: Public Figure Bentley Theater, Hopkins Center for the Arts

An immersive solo show about fame, the attention economy and babyccinos—a drink you probably didn't know existed but won't be able to forget.

Public Figure is an immersive solo show about fame, the attention economy and babyccinos—a drink you probably didn't know existed but will soon never be able to forget. Written by Dylan Marron. Performed by Dylan Marron and, potentially, you.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:30 pm - 9:30 pm New York Theatre Workshop: Los Feliz Daryl Roth Studio Theater

Two Latina mothers form a bond. Can it withstand the differences that divide them?

Irene and Emilia quickly connect over common ground: they're both Latina, working mothers, against giving soda to toddlers, and could use a helping hand. When Irene hires Emilia as a live-in nanny, Emilia gains Irene as an ally in her immigration process. Housed under one roof, they become company to each other's needs, wants and dreams. As their lives intertwine, can they transcend the divergent realities they were born into and now face?

This event is free but tickets are required.

Get more info here.

Add to Google Calendar
Aug 1 – Aug 29, 2026 Upper Valley Photographers 4th Annual Open Call Exhibition AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 11:00 am · Free

Call for Entries

The Upper Valley Photographers invite you to participate in the 2026 Third Floor Open Call Exhibition at Gallery 3 @ AVA. This non-juried exhibition is open to all members of the Upper Valley Photographers group and highlights a wide range of photographic styles, techniques, and subject matter.

Whether you’re presenting new work or revisiting pieces from your archive, this exhibition is a great opportunity to connect with fellow photographers and share your work with a broader audience.

(Photo Credit: The Great Salt Plains, OK, by Ellen Jonsson)

Submission Rules:

  • All work must be created by the artist. The exposure that forms the basis of the print must have been made by the artist.
  • Any further manipulation or printing must also be completed by the artist.
  • A maximum of two submissions per artist.
  • Finished work (including mat/frame) must be no smaller than 5”x7” and no larger than 26” wide.
  • All work must be ready to hang (matted or framed).
  • Limited to the first 30 submissions on a first-come, first-served basis. If space is limited, the curator may select only one piece per artist for exhibition.

Artwork Guidelines:

  • Image(s) of your work
  • Title(s)
  • Medium
  • Price (if applicable)
  • Artist statement or brief biography

Important Dates:

Deadline for Submissions: July 10, 2026

Prints Due: July 20–24, 2026 (bring to AVA’s main office)

Exhibition: August 1–29, 2026

Location: Gallery 3 @ AVA (Third Floor), AVA Gallery and Art Center

Entrance Fee: $35 per submission

Submission Link: https://www.artgalleria.com/portal/b37ed07

Sunday, August 2, 2026

10:00 am Wood Carving: Carved Animals | 10 am-1 pm AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

$0–$100

Instructor: Eva Sturm-Gross

In this three-hour hands-on workshop, artist Eva Sturm-Gross will guide participants through carving small animal figures from basswood, using basic hand carving tools such as knives and gauges. Designed to be approachable for beginners, the class covers tool safety, wood selection, and fundamental carving techniques, with an emphasis on hands-on experimentation. Come create in a supportive, focused studio environment where students can work at their own pace and leave with a finished (or well-started) carved animal and deeper appreciation for this traditional craft.

Add to Google Calendar
2:00 pm Sounds of Latin America Wind Ensemble Concert Chosen Vale Performance Center, Enfield

Sounds of Latin America

Wind Ensemble Concert

Sunday, August 2, 2026

2:00 pm

Chosen Vale Performance Center

Enfield Shaker Museum

410 NH Route 4A

Enfield, NH 03748

Please join us for a special concert event to benefit Enfield Shaker Museum’s Chosen Vale Performance Center. The Sounds of Latin America Wind Ensemble Concert program features 50 musicians from across New England, is free and open to the public, with 100% of all donations at the door going toward updates to the facility.

The all-volunteer Shaker Wind Ensemble is staffed by current and retired NH music educators, professional musicians, and community members. The group was formed in 2024 to help raise funds for the Enfield Shaker Museum’s renovation of the Chosen Vale Performance Center. We are excited to return for a second performance this summer.They will be performing Michael Gandolfi’s Vientos y Tangos, Shelley Hanson’s Islas y Montanas, and Aaron Copland’s El Salon Mexico. under the direction of Eileen Maderios.

We hope you will join us to enjoy this hour of music in the Upper Valley’s newest performance space!

Reservations not required. Plenty of free parking. The concert will be followed by a reception in the Performance Center.

Suggested donation $15.00. All proceeds go to support the ongoing renovation of the Chosen Vale Performance Center.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
4:00 pm - 6:30 pm NYTW Screening: Hadestown Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

The original cast reunites for this live theater capture of Anaïs Mitchell's Tony-winning musical, partly developed at Dartmouth with the New York Theater Workshop.

Hop Audiences got an early look at Hadestown during the New York Theater Workshop summer residency in 2016, before it went on to win eight Tony Awards, including Best Musical and Best Director for Rachel Chavkin. This live theater capture of that Broadway sensation features the five original principals: Reeve Carney as Orpheus, André De Shields as Hermes, Amber Gray as Persephone, Eva Noblezada as Eurydice and Patrick Page as Hades. 

The genre-defying new musical blends modern American folk music with New Orleans-inspired jazz to reimagine a sweeping ancient tale about the costs of industrialization and how artists can help us bring the world back into balance. Following two intertwining love stories—that of young dreamers Orpheus and Eurydice, and that of King Hades and his wife Persephone—Hadestown invites audiences on a hell-raising journey to the underworld and back.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
