Add to Google Calendar
4:00 pm Golden Hour in the Garden with Barr Hill Enfield Shaker Museum

$25 – $45

Golden Hour in the Garden with Barr Hill

Sunday, August 2 | 4:00–6:00 PM

The Enfield Shaker Museum is delighted to partner with Barr Hill Gin for a special Bee’s Knees Celebration!

Join us on August 2 from 4:00–6:00 PM for Golden Hour in the Garden with Barr Hill, an afternoon of relaxation, creativity, and flavor. Stroll through the gardens, enjoy the beauty of the season, and experience the important connection between pollinators, plants, and community.

What’s Included

Each ticket purchased includes:

  • One drink ticket for a specialty cocktail or non-alcoholic mocktail featuring lavender syrup from Pumpkin Blossom Farm
  • A curated snack box
  • A handcrafted lavender sachet to take home

Guided Garden Painting Class

Enhance your afternoon by participating in a two-hour guided painting class with local artist Shannon Bender. Shannon will lead participants step-by-step through creating a simple landscape painting. No experience is necessary, and all materials are included.

Painting Class

  • Museum Members $40 per person – Non-Members $45 per person
  • Limited to 16 participants
  • Advance registration required

The garden bar will be open from 4:00–6:00 PM serving specialty cocktails and mocktails. Additional beverages and culinary treats will be available for purchase. Cash and credit cards will be accepted.

A Great Drink. A Greater Purpose.

Barr Hill Gin is committed to supporting pollinator habitats across the country.

Simply post a photo of your cocktail on social media and tag @barrhillgin.

For every tagged post, Barr Hill funds one square foot of new pollinator habitat—tracked, verified, and reported back to participants.

No complicated sign-up. No grand commitment. Just a great drink connected to something that matters.

Come spend a summer afternoon in the garden celebrating creativity, community, and the bees that make it all possible.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
4:59pm Music in My Pockets: Family Fun in Folk Music Dana meeting House 288 Dana Hill Rd New Hampton NH 03256

Singing games, accessible "pocket instruments" like spoons and dancing puppets, tall tales, funny songs, old songs and songs kids teach each other in the playground-all "traditional" in that they have been passed down the generations by word of mouth-will be seen, heard and learned. We will revisit 1850 or 1910 in a New England town, with families gathered around the kitchen hearth, participating in timeless, hearty entertainment: a glimpse into how America amused itself before electricity. This program is recommended for adults and children ages 6 and above. 

Limited handicap parking near ramp. There is no electricity or running water but there is an outhouse and a porta potty. Please be respectful of the hand built bix pews inside this historic building when you enter to sit.
Humanities to GoJeff WarnerDana Meeting House AssociationBlair Folts, 603 733 7050
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Monday, August 3, 2026

9:30 am - 3:00 pm The Poetry of Cinema: a workshop Literary Arts Bridge (7 Lebanon St. Suite 105)

In this workshop, we'll explore the creative crossroads between poetry and cinema, and learn how poets and filmmakers use image, language, and memory to create emotionally resonant work. Drawing on poems, screenplay excerpts, and film clips, we'll examine how a single lyric image can become the foundation for character, scene, and narrative, with a special focus on crafting the opening pages of a screenplay. Whether you're interested in writing poetry, screenplays, or both, you'll leave with practical strategies for getting started, shaping your first pages, and approaching revision with fresh eyes.

SCHEDULE
9:30-10:30 AM - John Wall Barger: Exploring Tarkovsky’s 1966 Soviet-Russian film Andrei Rublev, and poems by Marie Howe, to show how a film is like a poem, and how watching great films can inspire us to write poems.

11:00 AM-12:00 PM - Elena Karina Byrne: Poetry's Cinematic Propositions; how poetry and film share key elements of discovery, utilizing the transformative ingredients of language, action, and image. 

12:00-1:30 PM - Lunch provided. 

1:30-3:00 PM - Perry Janes: How the lyric image crosses from poetry to film, and how that can serve as an originating point for your screenplay

John Wall Barger is the author of seven books of poems and a collection of essays. His latest book of poems, Resurrection Pie, just came out with LSU Press. He’s a contract editor for Frontenac House, lives in Vermont, and lectures in the Writing Program at Dartmouth College.

Elena Karina Byrne is a screenwriter, essayist, reviewer, multi-media artist, and editor. She also works as a freelance lecturer and national arts programs curator. Author of five collections of poetry including If This Makes You Nervous (Omnidawn), No, Don’t (What Books Press), Squander (Omnidawn), MASQUE (Tupelo Press), and The Flammable Bird (Zoo Press/Tupelo Press), Elena is a Pushcart Prize and Best American Poetry recipient. Her poems, reviews, essays, and interviews can be found in POETRY, The Kenyon Review, The Paris Review, Poetry International, Poetry Daily, Ploughshares, BOMB, Academy of American Poets Poem-A-Day, and elsewhere.

Perry Janes is the author of the poetry collection FIND ME WHEN YOU’RE READY (Curbstone Books/Northwestern University Press, 2024), winner of the Eric Hoffer Award for Poetry. A recipient of the Pushcart Prize, his poems and short stories have appeared in POETRY, Electric Literature, Poem-a-Day, Best New Poets, The Threepenny Review, Zyzzyva, The Michigan Quarterly Review, Subtropics, Prairie Schooner, Beloit Poetry Journal, The Adroit Journal, The Missouri Review, and others. He earned his MFA in Poetry from Warren Wilson College, and his BA from the University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, where he was a 5-time recipient of the Hopwood Award.

Vievee Francis is the author of five books of poetry: Blue-Tail Fly (2007), Horse in the Dark (2010), Forest Primeval (2016), The Shared World (2023), and the forthcoming Cleaning the Houses of the Dead. Born in West Texas and raised in Metropolitan Detroit, Francis has received numerous awards and recognition for her work including a Guggenheim Fellowship (2024), a Vermont Book Award (2023), the Aiken Taylor Award for Modern American Poetry (2021), the Kingsley Tufts Poetry Award (2017), and the Hurston Wright Legacy Award (2016). Francis, along with composer Jonathan Berger and artist Enrico Riley, wrote the libretto for the transdisciplinary opera The Ritual of Breath which premiered in 2022. She has served as an Associate Editor for Callaloo and currently holds the Burlington Northern Foundation Professorship in Creative Writing at Dartmouth College.

Literary Arts Bridge

10:00 am Storytime in the Garden Enfield Shaker Museum Garden

Free

Storytime in the Garden

with the Enfield Public Library

Monday, August 3 @ 10 am

Join us for a special outdoor Storytime in the Garden in partnership with the Enfield Public Library at the Enfield Shaker Museum Garden.

On Monday, August 3 at 10:00 a.m., librarian Miss Kate will lead stories and songs for children under 5 and their caregivers in the beautiful surroundings of the Enfield Shaker Museum Children’s Garden.

After storytime, children will create a simple garden-themed ornament to take home, then explore our new Touch, See, Smell Sensory Garden and Children’s Pollinator Garden, where they can discover plants, flowers, and pollinators through hands-on exploration.

This event is free and open to the public, and all families are welcome. We hope you’ll join us for a morning of stories, creativity, and garden fun!

Rain Location: School room in the Ministry House

Aug 3 – Aug 7, 2026 ages 12-16: Figurative Carving Intensive | 9 am-12 pm AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 9:00 am · $0–$281

1 week | August 3-10 | 9 am-12 pm

Instructor: Eva Sturm-Gross

In this course students will learn how to carve wood by hand safely and effectively. This class is designed to not only teach students the mechanics of carving but to learn to think three dimensionally in sculpture. We will work with a variety of hand carving tools and learn how to sharpen and maintain them. Students will start by carving small animal figures before designing and executing their own project.

Add to Google Calendar
Aug 3 – Aug 7, 2026 Middle Schoolers: Forging and Other Life Skills | 9:30 am-3:30 pm AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 9:30 am · $0–$595

M-F, Aug 3-7 | 9:30 am-3:30 pm

Instructor: Sabrina Fadial

In these interactive, experiential classes, middle school-aged campers will make, experiment, and problem-solve with their hands. Over one week, students will transform raw metal into finished objects, building technical skills alongside resilience, teamwork, and self-confidence.

*This class is designed for 5th-8th graders (must have graduated from 5th grade).

This class meets in AVA's metal studio.

Tiered Pricing: $595, $480, $245, or pay-what-you-can

Sabrina Fadial is an artist, educator, and mentor with a decades-long professional art practice and over 30 years of experience teaching and working with young people. Her work as a sculptor and metals artist informs her dynamic, hands-on teaching approach that centers experiential learning, curiosity, and creative problem-solving.

Sabrina has taught art, design, welding, metals, and blacksmithing to students from middle school through graduate school in schools, museums, afterschool programs, and community organizations. Deeply committed to youth empowerment—especially underserved communities, inner-city students, and girls and female+ students—she creates collaborative learning environments where students build technical skills, confidence, and resilience through making.

She currently serves as Executive Director of the T.W. Wood Museum in Montpelier while continuing to teach and mentor young people in welding and metal arts. Her commitment to access and equity aligns perfectly with AVA's inclusionary pricing approach.

Add to Google Calendar

Tuesday, August 4, 2026

9 am - 12 pm New Faculty Orientation to Teaching audience: Faculty, Postdoc, Students-Graduate Zoom

Dartmouth's New Faculty Orientation to Teaching provides useful information and introductions to help you get started successfully in your teaching role at Dartmouth. Facilitated by the Dartmouth Center for the Advancement of Learning (DCAL) and Learning Design & Innovation (LDI), the Orientation to Teaching is designed to complement other welcome and orientation sessions (with your department, the Dean of Faculty, and Office of Human Resources) that you might attend as you begin your work at the College. At Dartmouth's New Faculty Orientation to Teaching, participants will:

  • Discover resources and support for teaching
  • Hear from faculty and students about teaching and learning at Dartmouth
  • Meet people who can support your teaching
  • Meet other new faculty 
  • Get excited and more relaxed about teaching at Dartmouth This event is intended for faculty new to Dartmouth each year, or who will begin teaching at Dartmouth in the academic year.

If you are unsure whether this event is suitable for you, please contact us to inquire at dcal@dartmouth.edu. This orientation will take place on Zoom. Please register to receive meeting details.

Register here. (opens new tab)

Faculty, Postdoc, Students-GraduateDartmouth Center for the Advancement of Learning (DCAL), Learning Design and Innovation
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
3:30 pm - 4:30 pm Physics and Astronomy Quantum Seminar - Dr. Woo-Joong Kim, Seattle University academic Wilder 202 and Zoom

Abstract: In this talk, I will provide an overview of my undergraduate research program at Seattle University (SU). Over the past decade, I have developed a suite of high-precision experimental techniques for testing short-range gravity and performing dynamic force microscopy, including an autocollimator, a Michelson interferometer, and phase-sensitive detection methods. These techniques have been progressively integrated into undergraduate biophysics research, where they are uniquely suited to address challenging biological questions.

In collaboration with researchers in the Biology Department at SU and the Fred Hutch Cancer Center in Seattle, our current goal is to develop specialized experimental tools to investigate the dynamics of BrxR, a genetic regulator that controls the expression of the BREX defense system.Hosted by Professor Roberto Onofrio Zoom Meeting Link: https://dartmouth.zoom.us/j/98435866316
Physics & Astronomy Department

5:00 pm - 6:30 pm Poetry Reading: John Wall Barger, Elena Karina Byrne, & Perry Janes Literary Arts Bridge (7 Lebanon St. Suite 105)

John Wall Barger is a poet, essayist, translator, and the author of seven books of poems and a collection of essays titled The Elephant of Silence: Essays on Poetics and Cinema. His latest book of poems, Resurrection Pie, just came out with LSU Press. He’s a contract editor for Frontenac House, lives in Vermont, and lectures in the Writing Program at Dartmouth College.

Elena Karina Byrne is a screenwriter, essayist, reviewer, multi-media artist, and editor. She also works as a freelance lecturer and national arts programs curator. Author of five collections of poetry including If This Makes You Nervous (Omnidawn), No, Don’t (What Books Press), Squander (Omnidawn), MASQUE (Tupelo Press), and The Flammable Bird (Zoo Press/Tupelo Press), Elena is a Pushcart Prize and Best American Poetry recipient. Her poems, reviews, essays, and interviews can be found in POETRY, The Kenyon Review, The Paris Review, Poetry International, Poetry Daily, Ploughshares, BOMB, Academy of American Poets Poem-A-Day, and elsewhere.

Perry Janes is the author of the poetry collection FIND ME WHEN YOU’RE READY (Curbstone Books/Northwestern University Press, 2024), winner of the Eric Hoffer Award for Poetry. A recipient of the Pushcart Prize, his poems and short stories have appeared in POETRY, Electric Literature, Poem-a-Day, Best New Poets, The Threepenny Review, Zyzzyva, The Michigan Quarterly Review, Subtropics, Prairie Schooner, Beloit Poetry Journal, The Adroit Journal, The Missouri Review, and others. He earned his MFA in Poetry from Warren Wilson College, and his BA from the University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, where he was a 5-time recipient of the Hopwood Award.

Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Banjos, Bones, and Ballads Moultonborough Public Library 4 Holland Street Moultonborough NH 03254

Traditional songs, rich in local history and a sense of place, present the latest news from the distant past. They help us to interpret present-day life with an understanding of the working people who built our country. Tavern songs, banjo tunes, 18th century New England hymns, sailor songs, and humorous stories about traditional singers and their songs highlight this informative program by Jeff Warner. 

Accessible parking and entrance. Assistive Listening System available.
Humanities to GoJeff WarnerMoultonborough Public LibraryJosianne Fitzgerald, 603-476-8895
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Wednesday, August 5, 2026

10:00am The History of Agriculture as Told by Barns Greenfield Historical society 828 Forest Road Greenfield, NH NH 03047

Barns can tell us a great deal about the history of agriculture in New Hampshire. In the colonial period, New Hampshire was a rural, agrarian state and small subsistence farms dotted the landscape. An important part of these farmsteads was the barn, which housed animals and stored crops. Early barns used traditional building methods and followed the English barn style, with a low pitched roof and doors under the eaves. As time went on, the farms expanded to accommodate changes in agriculture. This presentation will follow the progression of barn styles that evolved to handle the increased productivity required to meet the needs of a growing population and respond to changes in society caused by the railroad and the Industrial Revolution. John C. Porter, author of Preserving Old Barns: Preventing the Loss of a Valuable Resource, will demonstrate how these majestic barn structures represent Yankee ingenuity, hard work, and skilled craftsmanship, as well as providing a link to our past that adds to the state's scenic beauty. 

This will be an in-person program with a light lunch available. This program is being coordinated with the dedication of our new barn/carriage shed, honoring a fine former Greenfield resident, Carl Inglestrom, who spent his career in the field of agriculture. The venue will be at least partially outside, weather permitting.
Humanities to GoJohn C. PorterGreenfield Historical SocietyAdele Hale, 603-547-3403
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6 pm - 7 pm Publishing Talk: Catherine Tudish & Peter Orner Literary Arts Bridge (7 Lebanon St. Suite 105)

Born into an air force family, Catherine Tudish grew up moving from one place to the next, living in England and France, as well as both coasts and the heartland of America. After receiving a B.A. in English from Southern Illinois University and a Ph.D. in American Studies from St. Louis University, she began work as a teacher and journalist. Her first book, a collection of stories called Tenney’s Landing, was published by Scribner in 2005. Since then, she has published the novel American Cream, as well as numerous articles and essays in regional newspapers and magazines. Her latest book, A Thousand Souls: A Novel in Stories, will be published by Acre Books in April 2026. Catherine has taught writing and literature at George Washington University, Harvard, Dartmouth, and the Bread Loaf School of English. She lives in a village in central Vermont and teaches a community writing workshop at the local library.

Born in Chicago, Peter Orner is the author of eight acclaimed books, including The Gossip Columnist's Daughter; Maggie Brown & Others; Love and Shame and Love; Esther Stories, finalist for the PEN/Hemingway Award; and Am I Alone Here?, a finalist for the National Book Critics Circle Award. His work has appeared in The New Yorker, the Paris Review, The Believer, and The Best American Short Stories, and received four Pushcart Prizes. A former Guggenheim fellow and recipient of the Rome Prize from the American Academy of Arts and Letters, Orner is professor of Creative Writing at Dartmouth College. He lives with his family in Vermont, where he's also a volunteer firefighter.

Literary Arts Bridge

6:00 pm Free Outdoor Concert – The Ferns Family Band Enfield Shaker Museum

2026 Free Outdoor Concert Series

The Ferns Family Band

Wednesday, August 5, 2026

Live music starts at 7 pm. Vending begins at 6 pm.

Join us outside the historic Stone Machine Shop for a FREE outdoor concert with The Ferns Family Band!

The Ferns Family Band is a trio fronted by award winning singer Mara Ferren, who never fails to impress with her seemingly effortless vocal prowess. Mara is supported by her brother Quinlan Ferren the backbone of the band on keys who masterfully juggles all piano, organ, bass, percussion and orchestral sounds. Father Justin Ferren, a seasoned musician spices up the spaces in between with tasty electric and acoustic guitar as well as percussion and live looping. The Ferns perform a mix of covers and originals spanning pop, folk, blues, soul and country sharing the magic of live music throughout New England and abroad.

Bring your chairs and blankets and enjoy this free outdoor concert with The Ferns Family Band at the Enfield Shaker Museum Stone Machine Shop.

Beer, wine, soda, and snacks will be available to purchase during the concert. (No private alcohol allowed.)

Enfield House of Pizza will be on site.

Live music will start at 7 pm. Vending will begin at 6 pm.

Rain or shine (we will move inside in case of rain.) Plenty of free parking.

THANK YOU TO OUR SPONSORS:

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Sandown Town Hall 320 Main St. Sandown NH 03873

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Plenty of parking. Handicap accessible ramp.
Humanities to GoMary AdamsSandown Heritage CommissionFran Rosenau, fsrosenau@mygsc.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Putting Human Faces on the Textile Industry: The Workers of the Amoskeag Manufacturing Company Canaan Meetinghouse 465 Canaan St Canaan NH 03741

Daily life for the Amoskeag Manufacturing Company's textile worker was not easy. Robert Perreault sheds light on how people from a variety of European countries as well as from French Canada made the transition from an agrarian to an industrial society and how that change affected families, cultures, the nature of work, and relationships among workers themselves. 

Humanities to GoRobert B. PerreaultCanaan Historical SocietyJohn Bergeron, 603-252-9622

Add to Google Calendar

Thursday, August 6, 2026

6:00pm Who Was John Stark? Pease Public Library 1 Russell Street Plymouth NH 03264

While many are familiar with the state motto “live free or die,” few might know about the man, General John Stark, who uttered these words. In this presentation, George Morrison reveals the man – a ransomed captive, Ranger officer, road-builder, lumberman, husband and father, repeatedly called-on to serve but often clashing with authority -- whose phrase has become synonymous with our state. 

Humanities to GoGeorge MorrisonPease Public LibraryBecky White, rwhite@peasepubliclibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Ernest Harold Baynes: New Hampshire’s Own Doctor Dolittle Flanders-Osborne Museum 74 Main St Sunapee NH 03782

Ernest Harold Baynes is fondly remembered in New Hampshire as our own Doctor Dolittle. To the rest of the nation, he is known as an important early 20th century wildlife conservationist. A self-taught naturalist, he wrote and lectured extensively on animal behavior, bird and bison restoration, the role of animals in WWI, and vivisection. Baynes shared his New Hampshire home with tame bear, fox, boar, bison, and wolf. He fought to end the trade in bird feathers for fashion and established one of the first bird sanctuaries in America. While serving as conservator to the Corbin Park bison herd, he convinced President Theodore Roosevelt to support the preserving of the American bison from extinction through the founding of the American Bison Society. Baynes’ work inspires us to consider how we intervene to help wildlife recover from the harms inflicted on them by humans. This illustrated lecture includes archival Baynes photographs from the Plainfield Historical Society collection.

Humanities to GoMary KronenwetterSunapee Historical SocietyBecky Rylander sunapeehistory@gmail.com, 603-763-9872
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Franklin Public Library 310 Central Street Franklin NH 03235

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

ADA compliant with elevator off rear parking lot

Saturday, August 8, 2026

9:00 am Yoga in the Garden Enfield Shaker Museum Garden

$15 – $20

August 8th at 9:00am
Yoga in the Garden

Join us on Saturday, August 8th at 9:00 a.m. for a peaceful and rejuvenating outdoor yoga experience. You will be  surrounded by nature and the tranquil atmosphere of the historic Shaker Village.  This is an all levels yoga class. This class is the perfect opportunity to slow down, reconnect, and begin your weekend with intention.

The class will be led by Caleigh, a Registered Yoga Teacher (RYT-500) and registered nurse. Caleigh brings a thoughtful and holistic approach to movement and wellness. Through mindful breathing, gentle stretching, and guided movement. Participants will be encouraged to cultivate balance, strength, flexibility, and relaxation. Whether you are new to yoga or have an established practice, this class will include modifications and options to help support your individual needs and comfort level.

This accessible yoga class is designed for participants who can comfortably get down to the ground and return to standing without assistance. Being able to stand unsupported, and bear weight on their hands, wrists, and knees will make this class more enjoyable.  Caleigh will provide guidance throughout the class to help participants move safely and confidently.

Please wear comfortable, stretchy clothing suitable for movement and bring a yoga mat or beach towel, along with water to stay hydrated. We also recommend arriving a few minutes early to settle into the garden setting and enjoy the peaceful surroundings before class begins.

In the event of inclement weather, the class will be moved indoors to the Stone Mill Building.

Come nurture your body, calm your mind, and enjoy a morning of wellness in one of the most beautiful settings in the Upper Valley.

Cost: $20 per person.- Non Member Pricce

15.00 Member Price

If you want to create a deeper appreciation for where food comes from, partake in meaningful conversations, or simply enjoy chill time outdoors, then Pizza Retreat is here to help you connect to what matters most.

Add to Google Calendar

Sunday, August 9, 2026

4:00 pm 2026 Mary Keane Chapel Summer Concert Series Enfield Shaker Museum

All dates: Aug 9, Aug 16, Aug 23, Sep 13

Mary Keane Chapel Summer Concert Series

Sunday, August 9, 2026

4:00 pm Start

All concerts will be held in the Mary Keane Chapel
Enfield Shaker Museum
447 NH Route 4A
Enfield, NH 03748
shakermuseum.org

Suggested donation $15.00. All concert donations support the ongoing restoration of the organ.

All concerts will be followed by a reception in the Museum Herb Garden (weather permitting) or in the Great Stone Dwelling.

Solo Organ Recital

Henry Danaher

Henry Danaher, Director of Music at the Church of Christ at Dartmouth College in Hanover, NH, and also serves as College Organist at Dartmouth College, will share a program of solo organ pieces, and collaboration with colleagues.  Enfield Shaker Museum is grateful that Henry is a champion of Enfield’s Casavant Organ, and an indispensable partner in recruiting musicians for this recital series.

Organ Restoration Update
Bob Waters and Richard Nickerson are continuing to address important restoration needs on the Casavant organ in the Museum’s Mary Keane Chapel. This fall and winter they removed and completely rebuilt the wind chest that receives and regulates the air pressure produced by the organ’s electric blower. This included replacing all the original leather work that controls the release of air to the organ pipes. In addition to maintaining the organ’s tuning, they have been repairing coupling mechanisms that have failed. Unfortunately the Casavant company no longer makes certain replacement parts, so some aspects of the restoration work requires creative problem solving. We are fortunate to have two craftsmen, well versed in the intricacies of Casavant organ construction, caring for this precious instrument.

Thank you for your support of their ongoing restoration work.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Digging Into Native History in New Hampshire Pease Public Library 1 Russell Street Plymouth NH 03264

Abenaki history has been reduced to near-invisibility as a result of conquest, a conquering culture that placed little value on the Indian experience, and a strategy of self-preservation that required many Abenaki to go "underground," concealing their true identities for generations to avoid discrimination and persecution. Robert Goodby reveals archaeological evidence that shows their deep presence here, inches below the earth's surface. 

Humanities to GoRobert GoodbyPease Public LibraryBecky White, rwhite@peasepubliclibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

StaffLibrary
Full details ↗
Aug 12 Sep 2 Oct 14
7:00 pm The Nail and the Thread: An Evening with Monochrom Productions Main Galleries | AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

$0–$25

The Nail and the Thread: How monochrom Built a World from Rubble and Trouble and Stays Restlessly Curious!

monochrom began in Vienna in 1993 as a scrappy fanzine born from punk, politics, science fiction, and dissatisfaction with a cyberculture already hardening into ideology. It grew, without anything resembling a master plan, into an internationally active art collective, film-production company, social incubator, and generator of context hacks. Its founder and artistic director, Johannes Grenzfurthner, has been called a "Leitnerd," a designation that rather neatly captures his dubious role in the overlapping worlds of art, technology, hacking, theory, and organized troublemaking.

For more than three decades, monochrom has manipulated audiences into participating in lies, invented realities, and temporary worlds, then fed productively on the emotions these situations produced. Its projects include Roboexotica, the world's leading festival for cocktail robotics; Arse Elektronika, a conference on sex and technology; experiments involving premature burial; fictional nations, improvised institutions, public interventions, performances, computer games, feature films, documentaries, and many other attempts to locate the correct weapon of mass distribution for an idea.

In this talk-performance, Grenzfurthner navigates the nails, loose threads, glorious mistakes, and temporary solidarities that have held monochrom's strange universe together. Rather than offering a tidy retrospective, he asks what happens when an art group refuses to settle into a single medium, identity, or respectable career path, and instead keeps building new worlds from whatever rubble and trouble happens to be available. It is a story about productive failure, political mischief, DIY culture, and the stubborn conviction that restless curiosity can still function as a method of resistance.

Thursday, August 13, 2026

8:30 am - 9:30 am Outdoor Yoga Series: Wake Up with Yoga Baker Lawn

Each class will open with a brief meditation followed by a gentle, yet energizing, flow that includes sun salutations, strong standing postures, and calming forward folds. We will conclude with a brief relaxation to help send you off on a day built on a foundation of presence and peace.

Bring your mat (or use one of ours), a towel, water and enjoy a gentle entrance to the day.

Friday, August 14, 2026

5:30 pm Conversations | Natasha Wein: If These Feet Could Fly Main Gallery- AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Free

This public event will take place in Natasha Wein's exhibition in the E.N. Wennberg Gallery, she will be speaking via Google Meets, Friday, August 14, 5:30-6:30 pm.

What do you do when the body you've built your art around stops working?

In 2019, Natasha Wein was diagnosed with Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome — a connective tissue disorder that ravaged her health, injured her spine, and took away the use of her hands. So she taped paper to the floor and learned to paint with her feet. Then, in 2024, while working on this very collection, she was struck by a car and sustained a traumatic brain injury. If These Feet Could Fly is what emerged anyway.

This collection of foot-made paintings doesn't just document survival — it asks the harder questions: How do you live a meaningful life when wounded beyond repair? How do you grieve who you used to be, and learn to love whoever you're becoming?

Through paint and paper on the floor, Natasha resurrects what remains, mourns what's gone, and lets her work reshape her into something whole — or at least, something legible.

Join us for an intimate evening with Natasha (via online with Google Meets) as she shares the story behind these paintings, the process of making them, and what it means to keep creating when everything keeps changing. This public event will take place in Natasha's exhibition in the E.N. Wennberg Gallery, she will be speaking via Google Meets.

Sunday, August 16, 2026

4:00pm Rally Round the Flag: The American Civil War Through Folksong North Wilmot Church 3 Breezy Hill Rd Wilmot NH 03287

Marek Bennett presents an overview of the American Civil War through the lens of period music. Audience members participate and sing along as the presenters explore lyrics, documents, and visual images from sources such as the Library of Congress. Through camp songs, parlor music, hymns, battlefield rallying cries, and fiddle tunes, Bennett examines the folksong as a means to enact living history, share perspectives, influence public perceptions of events, and simultaneously fuse and conserve cultures in times of change. Showcasing numerous instruments, the presenters challenge participants to find new connections between song, art, and politics in American history. 

Parking on site & along road; cell phone coverage is spotty in North Wilmot; Refreshments served after the program.
Humanities to GoMarek BennettAnn DavisAnn Davis, 603-526-6960 northwilmotchurch@gmail.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Monday, August 17, 2026

5:00 pm - 6:30 pm "Kingdom of Devils": Book Talk with Author Katherine Grandjean Haldeman 041

The Department of History welcomes guest professor Katherine Grandjean (Wellesley College) to present a lecture on her recent monograph, Kingdom of Devils: A Tale of Murder in the Shadow of the American Revolution (Random House, 2026).

Kingdom of Devils tells the story of a brutal series of murders on the Revolutionary frontier. In the late 1790s, brothers Wiley and Micajah Harp killed dozens of people in the borderlands of Kentucky and Tennessee. No one has ever really explained why they did it. Drawing from her research, Grandjean uses the Harp murders to reveal the dark side of the young United States’ independence. Unspooling the mystery of the Harps’ spree, this talk exposes the hidden, violent legacies of the revolutionary era.

Katherine Grandjean is Associate Professor of History at Wellesley College. The recipient of major research fellowships from the National Endowment for the Humanities, the American Council of Learned Societies, and American Antiquarian Society, among others, she is also the author of American Passage (Harvard, 2015).

History Department
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Tuesday, August 18, 2026

8 am - 9 am PhD Thesis Proposal: Emily Monroe academic Rm 035, ECSC

ZOOM LINK
Meeting ID: 921 5217 8697
Passcode: 105641

Abstract: Professional licensure asks engineers to hold paramount the safety, health, and welfare of the public. Engineering education has strong tools to measure what students can do and how they identify as engineers, but it has no validated instrument for the normative professional commitment that licensure embodies: the felt obligation to protect the public even when doing so is costly. This dissertation develops and validates the Professional Engineering Commitments Scale (PECS), a measure grounded in Meyer and Allen's conception of normative commitment, to address that gap.

PECS measures four constructs: Public Welfare, Internalized Values, Licensure Commitment, and AI Verification and Accountability, the last capturing the engineer's responsibility to verify the work of increasingly capable AI systems and to remain the accountable human in the loop. Items were generated through a theory-, practice-, and standards-grounded pipeline and refined using semantic network analysis. A panel of practicing and academic experts then reviewed the pool, yielding strong content validity (S-CVI/Ave = 0.951) and a retained 66-item exploratory pool.

The proposed work tests the scale's structure and meaning. Exploratory factor analysis will examine dimensionality, followed by confirmatory factor analysis and tests of reliability and measurement invariance over the following year. The capstone design experience, studied as a site of cognitive apprenticeship and professional formation, provides the setting for examining whether and how commitment forms.

Finally, the dissertation positions commitment as a practically useful variable. By linking PECS scores to workforce questions in AI-integrated, cyber-resilient practice, it asks how a validated measure of professional commitment might inform where and how the next generation of engineers is developed and directed. The contribution is a theoretically grounded, empirically validated instrument that lets the field measure a commitment it has long valued but never directly assessed, along with a demonstration of why that measurement matters for public welfare.

Thesis Committee: Solomon Diamond (Chair), Geoffrey Parker, Eugene Santos Jr., Idalis Villanueva Alarcón (Department of Engineering Education, University of Florida)

Thayer School of Engineering

12:15pm Discovering New England Stone Walls Tilton Senior Center 11 Grange Road Tilton New Hampshire 03276

Why are we so fascinated with stone walls? Kevin Gardner, author of The Granite Kiss, explains how and why New England came to acquire its thousands of miles of stone walls, the ways that they and other dry stone structures were built, how their styles emerged and changed over time, and their significance to the famous New England landscape. Along the way, Kevin occupies himself building a miniature wall or walls on a tabletop, using tiny stones from a five-gallon bucket. 

Senior Center meal served before program.

Learn about the text of the Declaration of Independence, who the key players were, the Enlightenment influence and if the values espoused in the Declaration are still relevant today. Participants will experience an unbiased look at the Declaration and receive valuable content to understand the influences and ideals of the document. They will be able to critically think about the document and have the knowledge of what it actually says. They will be able to form opinions and speak with conviction about what they learned.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Piermont Old Church Building 132 NH Rt10 Piermont NH 03779

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Humanities to GoMary AdamsPiermont Historical SocietyFred Shipman, 603.359.9845
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Last of the Granite State Rattlesnakes Alton Public Library 100 Main St. Alton NH 03809

Rattlesnakes were so common in colonial-era New Hampshire that dozens of places were named for them, ranging from the Massachusetts border to the White Mountains. They were so abundant some settlers could pay their property taxes with the bounties offered for killing them. Today, however, Crotalus horridus, the timber rattlesnake, is the state’s rarest native animal—only a handful are known to survive. State law protects them now, but that may not be enough to save them. Wildlife biologists have predicted this tiny remnant will die out without extraordinary intervention.

How did this feared and fascinating animal come to the very brink of extirpation in our state? This illustrated presentation maps how we got here through the social, political, and natural history of New Hampshire’s rattlesnakes. Historian and former newspaper reporter Dan Billin presents vivid, little-known stories and images from our centuries-long war on our rattlesnakes. Topics include who’s been bitten; the medicinal use of rattlesnake oil by the Shakers and others; the exploits of the “Rattlesnake King of New Hampshire”; the twentieth-century rattlesnake panic in the legislature; and the mysterious recent appearance of rattlesnakes with Pennsylvania genes. This program reviews what is and isn’t being done today to conserve our rattlesnakes, and the many obstacles to their survival.

There is parking on the street as well as a small parking lot. The entrance to the meeting room is in the back of the building at ground level.

Humanities to GoDan BillinAlton Historical SocietySandra Hammond 603-569-3745

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
11:00am African American Soldiers and Sailors of New Hampshire During the American Revolution Penacook Activity Room 76 Community Drive Penacook NH 03303

One of the most interesting aspects of the American Revolution is the role played by African Americans in the fight for independence. Both free African Americans and those that were enslaved were key in manning state militias and Continental Army units, as well as serving on the high seas in the Navy and on privately armed ships. Indeed, their service to the colonies was crucial in a conflict that lasted nearly seven years. Prohibited from serving in military units and largely considered "undesirable elements," how is it that these African-American soldiers came to fight for the cause of liberty, even when their own personal liberty was not guaranteed?

Glenn Knoblock examines the history of African-American soldiers' service during the war, including how and why they enlisted, their interaction with white soldiers, service on the battlefields, how they were perceived by the enemy and the officers under whom they served, and their treatment after the war. 

Humanities to GoGlenn KnoblockConcord Parks & RecreationLizzie Loughridge, lloughridge@concordnh.gov

Full details ↗
Aug 19 Sep 16 Oct 21
7:00pm Brewing in New Hampshire: An Informal History of Beer in the Granite State Quincy Bog Natural Area 131 Quincy Bog Road Rumney NH 03266

Glenn Knoblock explores the fascinating history of New Hampshire's beer and ale brewing industry from Colonial days, when it was home- and tavern-based, to today's modern breweries and brew pubs. Unusual and rare photos and advertisements document this changing industry and the state's earliest brewers, including the renowned Frank Jones. A number of lesser-known brewers and breweries that operated in the state are also discussed, including the only brewery owned and operated by a woman before the modern era. Illustrations present evidence of society's changing attitudes towards beer and alcohol consumption over the years. Whether you're a beer connoisseur or a "tea-totaler", this lecture will be enjoyed by adults of all ages.

Humanities to GoGlenn KnoblockQuincy Bog Natural AreaMarguerite A St. Laurent-Crowell, programs@quincybog.org

6:00 pm Taboo Tour – August 21st Enfield Shaker Museum

$45 – $50

Taboo Tour

at the Enfield Shaker Museum

Step beyond the traditional history tour and explore the darker, more mysterious side of Shaker life. The Enfield Shaker Museum presents a special series of three Taboo Tours, each examining fascinating and controversial topics from Shaker history, including scandal, murder, spiritualism, the afterlife, and the enduring question: Were the Shakers a cult?
Each evening features a unique theme, an interactive hands-on activity, and an opportunity to uncover stories rarely told on standard tours. Guests will also enjoy one complimentary cocktail, beer, or glass of wine with their ticket, along with light refreshments.
Tour Dates:
July 31 6:00-8:00
August 21 6:00-8:00
October 30 6:00-8:00
Cost: $50 per person
Capacity: Limited to 20 participants per tour
Join us for an unforgettable evening of history, mystery, and conversation as we uncover the secrets, beliefs, and controversies hidden within the Enfield Shaker communit

Saturday, August 22, 2026

10:00 am Rubber Stamp Patch Printing | 10 am-1 pm AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

$0–$37.50

Instructor: Fairy Stewart

In this hands-on printmaking workshop, participants will carve original designs into erasers or rubber blocks, then use them to print custom patches onto fabric. No experience necessary! Participants will choose their carving block, sketch or draw a design directly onto it, and learn basic lino-cutting techniques to bring their image to life. Once carved, they'll select an ink color and print their design onto fabric scraps — with the option to run rubber block prints through a printing press for paper prints as well. Come ready to trade fabric scraps with fellow participants and leave with one-of-a-kind patches to sew, iron, or attach to a jacket, bag, or whatever you bring along.

Participants should bring: a garment or item to attach patches to. All other materials provided.

Finished patches will be heat-set or available for pickup the following day.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Sunday, August 23, 2026

4:00pm A Walk Back in Time: The Secrets of Cellar Holes North Wilmot Church 3 Breezy Hill Rd Wilmot NH 03287

Northern New England is full of reminders of past lives: stone walls, old foundations, a century-old lilac struggling to survive as the forest reclaims a once-sunny dooryard. What forces shaped settlement, and later abandonment, of these places? Adair Mulligan explores the rich story to be discovered in what remains behind. See how one town has set out to create an inventory of its cellar holes, piecing together the clues in the landscape. Such a project can help landowners know what to do if they have archaeological sites on their land and help stimulate interest in a town's future through its past. 

Parking on site & along road; cell phone coverage is spotty in North Wilmot; Refreshments served after the program.
Humanities to GoAdair MulliganAnn DavisAnn Davis, 603-526-6960 northwilmotchurch@gmail.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Tuesday, August 25, 2026

5:30pm US@250: Big Watch Fuller Public Library 29 School Street Hillsboro New Hampshire 03244


Be a part of something BIG: Join US@250: Big Watch, a statewide series of community-driven programs that bring Granite Staters together to watch, discuss, and reflect on the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence and American Revolution.

 

Join us to commemorate and reflect upon the 250th anniversary with a film screening and discussion. Together, we’ll watch a short (30 min) excerpt from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION, a new film directed by Ken Burns, Sarah Botstein, and David Schmidt, followed by a community discussion. The featured segment from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION,“The Spirit of Service,” explores service as a core American value, from the nation’s founding through to the present day – examining volunteerism, civic participation, public leadership, and more.


Expert facilitator Mohamed Defaa will guide thoughtful conversations about “remembering together,” considering questions such as:

    • What moments from our past define who we are and point to a shared future? 
    • What are the key historical moments in your town’s or community’s history? 
    • How does focusing on these moments shape your sense of your community’s future?

 

This program will last approximately 1.5 hours (30 minutes for film screening, 1 hour for facilitated discussion). Please make sure you have appropriate technology for the film screening, including a strong internet connection, large screen, and sound system. Please contact programs@nhhumanities.org for the link to the themed excerpt. 

Big Watch programs are made possible by a partnership between New Hampshire Humanities and New Hampshire PBS.

 

"It's like being a part of history."
-Big Watch participant

Parking for the library is across the street. Handicap lift is available, as well as 2 handicap parking spots.
Humanities to GoSimohamed DefaaFuller Public LibrarySamantha Gallo, 603-464-3595

6:00pm Granite State Gallery: New Hampshire Art and Artists through the Years Virtual Virtual Virtual 03745

New Hampshire has attracted and inspired artists since the colonial era. What is distinctive about the art made here? This program will consider works by itinerant and folk painters, landscape artists drawn to the state's scenic vistas, and modern artists that adopted bold styles to depict everyday life in the Granite State. Augustus Saint-Gaudens, Childe Hassam, and Maxfield Parrish are some of the artists discussed in this program. 

This will be a Zoom webinar that will later be shared on our website. Please register for the Zoom webinar here.

Wednesday, August 26, 2026

12 pm - 1 pm Online Info Session: Master of Energy Transition Online via Zoom

Accelerate Your Impact on the Clean Energy Transition

Register for the online Info Session

The world is building a cleaner, more resilient energy future — and that shift needs people who can bridge technology, business, and policy. Dartmouth’s Master of Energy Transition (MET) degree program is designed to train exactly those leaders.

Join us for a live online info session to see what makes the MET different, and find out if it's the right next step for you.

What to expect:

  • Meet the people behind the program — our faculty director, program director, and MET team
  • Learn about the MET faculty
  • Take an inside look at our interdisciplinary curriculum and project-based courses
  • Discover how we connect you with expert mentors through Dartmouth’s powerful alumni network
  • Get a preview of our career treks, where MET students meet energy leaders on the ground in Houston and San Francisco
  • Walk away with the full picture on applying — process, tuition, and financial aid


We'll close with a live Q&A, so bring your questions!

💡 Bonus: Attend the session, and we'll waive your MET application fee.

Save your spot and take the first step toward powering the future.

Register for the online Info Session

1:30 pm - 2:30 pm Bingo! Welcome to Records Management 101 audience: Staff Berry 180B The LINK, Baker-Berry Library

Think you know your retention schedules? Play bingo using the General Retention Schedule, build familiarity with the tool, and make smarter keep-or-delete decisions.

🎉 Join in person for:
• Real daubers
• Free popcorn
• Limited-edition lenticular stickers
• A chance to win a $20 Still North gift card

Each bingo square is a question you'll answer using the General Retention Schedule (GRS), Dartmouth's guide to records common across campus, which will be accessible during the session.

📋 Sample square: How long must waivers be retained before deletion? Four years.

There will also be some time to field questions about how the GRS applies to your work.

Please register here.

StaffLibrary
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
2 pm - 4 pm Growing Up Jewish in Communist Budapest Osher at Dartmouth 1 Court St. Lebanon, NH 03766

In this gripping lecture, Judit Price will present a short historical background of the persecution and survival of the Hungarian Jewish population originating from the 11th century, and their consequent progression into the 21st century, including an overview of education, culture, and commerce. Judit will then focus on her life surviving under a Communist authoritarian regime, its many challenges, creating a stable life under threat, and how her family created an atmosphere of optimism enabling them to thrive within the limits of Communist rule.

Before retirement, Judit Price was a career transition consultant/coach and motivational speaker in private practice for over 35 years. She holds a double Masters Degree in Counseling and Coaching.

She coached and counseled mid-level professionals to senior level executives in career direction/career transition, and developing an online presence on social media. She was an in-demand speaker for various women’s organizations in the greater Boston area, and led workshops and training seminars for other trainers in employment and world-of-work related topics. She frequently appeared in various local TV and radio stations in MA and NH as a career expert.

She is married with three children and four granddaughters and retired to Naples, Florida, spending her time as a volunteer to various nonprofit organizations including the Naples Jewish Federation, Naples Holocaust Museum, Hadassah, and others. Judit is also a Holocaust survivor and can thank her life to Raul Wallenberg, a Swedish Diplomat who saved thousands of Jews from the gas chambers.

COST:
Osher Members: $10
Non-Members: $15

Learn More and Register

Osher Lifelong Learning Institute at Dartmouth

5:00 pm 10X10=AVA Benefit & Silent Auction Linda Roesch Visual Art Gallery | AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

$25–$100

Mark your calendars for AVA’s 2026 10x10= AVA Silent Auction and Fundraiser!

This pop-up benefit exhibition is on view between Monday, August 20th - Thursday, August 27th. On August 28 we host a silent auction and social hour where you can meet the artists, bid on and purchase their work, and celebrate AVA's remarkable community of support.

This is a rare opportunity to see over 120 works from New England artists, thanks to these generously donated artworks! Don’t miss a chance to bid on and take home artwork by many remarkable artists, among them: Louise Hamlin, Esme Thompson, Patrick Dunfey, Tom Ferrara, Meg McLean, Andrea Kantrowitz, and Helen Shulman.

$100 per person

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Saturday, August 29, 2026

9 am - 12 pm Gertrude Stein: Paris, Modernism, and a Family Connection Auditorium E, Dartmouth Hitchcock Medical Center One Medical Center Drive, Lebanon, NH 03766
Step into the legendary Paris salon of Gertrude Stein, where modern art was born and literary revolutionaries gathered. Nicholas Stein — documentary producer, author, and grandson of Gertrude Stein's favorite first cousins — brings his two-part presentation to Osher at Dartmouth after sold-out California engagements. Through family insights and cultural history, Nick will illuminate the woman who became one of the 20th century’s most influential figures. He’ll show how Gertrude’s radical experiments with language transformed literature, how her salon became the creative epicenter for Picasso, Hemingway, Matisse, and countless others, and why her fearless approach to art and life still resonates today. No background in modernist literature is needed for this presentation—just curiosity about a brilliant, unconventional woman who changed how we think about words, art, and creativity itself.

Nicholas Stein is a television producer (Border Wars, National Geographic; series for Discovery, PBS, Smithsonian, and A&E), author, and lifelong learning educator. Through a direct family connection — his grandparents were Gertrude Stein’s favorite first cousins — he brings extraordinary primary material to life, including rare 16mm home movies, personal correspondence, and stories passed down across three generations.

The presentation transports audiences inside the legendary Paris salon at 27 rue de Fleurus, where Picasso, Matisse, and Hemingway gathered, and explores Stein’s profound influence on both literature and the visual arts. As one attendee described it: “art history, biography, and intimate family memoir woven into one unforgettable experience.”

David Bisno, M.D. (Harvard, Dartmouth, Washington University School of Medicine) has led dynamic discussion programs for lifelong learners since 1993, across the U.S. and internationally.

Saturdays, August 29 and September 5
9:00 AM - 12:00 PM

Cost:
Osher Members: $65
Non-Members: $80

Learn More and Register

Osher Lifelong Learning Institute at Dartmouth

Sunday, August 30, 2026

9:15am Harnessing History: On the Trail of New Hampshire's State Dog, the Chinook Hybrid Starr King UU Fellowship 101 Fairgrounds Rd Plymouth NH 03264

This program looks at how dog sledding developed in New Hampshire and how the Chinook played a major role in this story. Explaining how man and his relationship with dogs won out over machines on several famous polar expeditions, Bob Cottrell covers the history of Arthur Walden and his Chinooks, the State Dog of New Hampshire. 

Building is accessible. Handicap parking in front of building on Fairgrounds Rd. More parking in back on Morgan Dr. No preregistration required. Zoom link on website starrkingfellowship.org

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Tuesday, September 1, 2026

6:00pm Brewing in New Hampshire: An Informal History of Beer in the Granite State Moultonborough Public Library 4 Holland Street Moultonborough NH 03254

Glenn Knoblock explores the fascinating history of New Hampshire's beer and ale brewing industry from Colonial days, when it was home- and tavern-based, to today's modern breweries and brew pubs. Unusual and rare photos and advertisements document this changing industry and the state's earliest brewers, including the renowned Frank Jones. A number of lesser-known brewers and breweries that operated in the state are also discussed, including the only brewery owned and operated by a woman before the modern era. Illustrations present evidence of society's changing attitudes towards beer and alcohol consumption over the years. Whether you're a beer connoisseur or a "tea-totaler", this lecture will be enjoyed by adults of all ages.

Co-sponsored by the Moultonborough Library and Moultonborough Historical Society. Assistive Listening System available. Accessible parking and entrance available.

Humanities to GoGlenn KnoblockMoultonborough Public LibraryJosianne Fitzgerald, 603-476-8895
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Benedict Arnold, Patriot (and Traitor) Exeter Town Hall 9 Front Street Exeter NH 03833

We all think we know the story of Benedict Arnold, the American Revolutionary War general who fought for the Continental Army but then defected to the British. Recalled mainly as a traitor for his 1780 defection, Arnold had risked his life and fortune for American freedom in courageous exploits between 1775 and 1778, when the dream of independence was at its most fragile. As an officer in the Continental Army, Arnold ably led American forces in desperate circumstances against impossible odds, in a blinding snowstorm, through a howling wilderness, and against the extraordinary might of the Royal Navy. George Morrison will take you on a journey through New England, Canada, and New York tracing the complex story of this infamous American icon. 

Humanities to GoGeorge MorrisonExeter Historical SocietyBarbara Rimkunas, Exeter Historical Society, 603-778-2335, info@exeterhistory.org

Sep 3 Sep 10 Sep 17 Sep 24 Oct 1 Oct 8 Oct 15 Oct 22
6:30pm The Ethics of AI Meredith Public Library 91 Main Street Meredith NH 03253

This program explores some of the most urgent moral challenges that Artificial Intelligence presents to society today. Topics include automation and job displacement, deepfakes and misinformation, the role of AI in education, implicit bias, autonomous systems (such as self-driving cars and drones), and the ethical implications of a potential technological singularity. After a brief introduction to applied ethics and the technology behind generative AI, we will examine each issue in turn, highlighting the core moral questions they raise and their significance for shaping sound policies and regulations. The session will conclude with an open discussion, giving attendees a chance to share perspectives and ask questions. Participants will come away with a clearer understanding of the ethical landscape of AI and the tools to think critically about its future impact.

Our building is accessible and there is parking around back off of High Street.
Humanities to GoKiki BerkMeredith Public LibraryErin Apostolos, erin@meredithlibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Friday, September 4, 2026

5:30 pm Conversations | Steve Arcone: Icescapes in the Abstract Main Gallery- AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Free

Steve Arcone will be in the galleries talking about is work in the exhibition, Icescapes in the Abstract: Images from New Hampshire Ponds, Friday, September 4, 5:30-6:30 PM

Steve Arcone merges science and art by using landscape macro-photography to capture the beauty of small-scale natural scientific phenomena and thus, to evoke subjective interpretations of them. Steve seeks abstract-appearing structural compositions, usually containing clues to the actual setting for the unfamiliar viewer. Inspired by his career researching and subsurface imaging Antarctic and Alaskan snow and ice, his main year-to-year specialty is cyclically occurring pond ice undergoing change during mid fall to early spring. The compositional motifs he mainly seeks are star-like structures grown from ice seeds on vegetation, smaller scale structures within them, and their change under stress from sunlight, temperature changes and gravity. The blue, silver and grey tones of ice are often enhanced by their contrast with the enduring and underlying fall vegetative coloration. His other ongoing macro-imaging projects are woodland brook-bottom sediments as seen through flowing water, structures within spider web strands, and the laminar flow of oil on water.

Biography | Steve Arcone up in Greenwich Village in New York City, where he attended Elisabeth Irwin High School. He graduated from Cornell University with B.S. and M.E.E. degrees, and earned his PhD from Thayer School of Engineering at Dartmouth College. Steve is a research geophysicist, having retired from the Cold Regions Lab in Hanover, New Hampshire where he specialized in using ground-penetrating radar to image subsurface structures within permafrost, glaciers, ice sheets and lake bottom sediments, with many publications in leading scientific journals. His research has taken him to Antarctica 12 times and more than 40 times to Alaska. Steve has also been adjunct professor at Dartmouth College, and the Universities of Maine and Connecticut. Since retirement he has sought to merge science and art in his landscape photography, including macro-imaging of pond and brook ice, water and oil flow, and spider webs. Steve is a juried member of The League of New Hampshire Craftsmen and The New Hampshire Photographic Society. Since 2023 his images have appeared in ten group exhibitions (3 of which included 10 or more images) in New England and New York, Northern Woodlands and Smoky Quartz (2 issues) magazines and in the Geologic Society of America 2024 calendar. He regularly exhibits at the League summer fair at Sunapee Ski Resort in Sunapee, New Hampshire, and his work is on display at several of the League galleries.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Saturday, September 5, 2026

10:30am Big House, Little House, Back House, Barn: The Connected Farm Buildings of New England Dublin Public Library 1114 Main Street Dublin New Hampshire 03444

Through architecture unique to northern New England, this illustrated talk focuses on several case studies that show how farmers converted their typical separate house and barns into connected farmsteads. Thomas Hubka's research in his award-winning book, Big House, Little House, Back House, Barn: The Connected Farm Buildings of New England, demonstrates that average farmers were, in fact, motivated by competition with farmers in other regions of America, who had better soils and growing seasons and fewer rocks to clear. The connected farmstead organization, housing equal parts mixed-farming and home-industry, was one of the collective responses to the competitive threat. 

Humanities to GoThomas HubkaDublin Public LibraryEmaline Bergeron, director@dplnh.org

Glenn Knoblock explores the fascinating history of New Hampshire's beer and ale brewing industry from Colonial days, when it was home- and tavern-based, to today's modern breweries and brew pubs. Unusual and rare photos and advertisements document this changing industry and the state's earliest brewers, including the renowned Frank Jones. A number of lesser-known brewers and breweries that operated in the state are also discussed, including the only brewery owned and operated by a woman before the modern era. Illustrations present evidence of society's changing attitudes towards beer and alcohol consumption over the years. Whether you're a beer connoisseur or a "tea-totaler", this lecture will be enjoyed by adults of all ages.

Add to Google Calendar

Tuesday, September 8, 2026

7:00pm Covered Bridges of New Hampshire - Past and Present Cornish Town Offices, 2nd Fl. 488 Town House Rd Cornish NH 03745

New Hampshire was once home to over 300 covered bridges. Today, there are over sixty authentic covered bridges in New Hampshire, forty-six of which are over a century old. These bridges exist solely because of the efforts of a small but powerful community that both recognized their significance and honored their tradition. Join author and photographer Kim Varney Chandler as she shares an overview of covered bridges in the Granite State, along with interesting facts she uncovered while researching her 2022 book, Covered Bridges of New Hampshire. Learn more about both existing and lost covered bridges in your area.

Refreshments will be provided.
Humanities to GoKim Varney ChandlerCornish Historical SocietyDavid Kruidenier, 919-673-0267; chs@cornishnh.net

Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Flight of Remembrance: From WWII in Europe to the U.S. Space Program Kimball Library 5 Academy Ave ATKINSON NH 03811

The true story of the speaker's family from the Axis side of WWII in Latvia, occupied Poland, and Germany—a story that includes two German veterans and provides a perspective seldom heard in the U.S. Though they did not support Hitler or join the Nazi Party, the speaker's father and grandfather, both technically skilled, were forced to serve in the German military after fleeing from Latvia to Germany before the first Soviet takeover of the Baltic States. As of 2022, the presentation includes new maps and parallels between the experiences of the speaker's family in WWII and those of Ukrainians today.

By giving a face and name to "the enemy," the presentation offers a seldom-shared perspective on the most devastating world conflict of all time, and sheds light on what it was like to live through war waged on home soil. Flight of Remembrance is a story of love, survival, and a lifelong dream for a career in aeronautical engineering that expanded to a key role in the emerging U.S. space program. 

Please register at Kimballlibrary.com in the Event Calendar section.
Humanities to GoMarina Dutzmann KirschAtkinson Historical SocietyRobert J Gustafson, 603.553.0531

Through architecture unique to northern New England, this illustrated talk focuses on several case studies that show how farmers converted their typical separate house and barns into connected farmsteads. Thomas Hubka's research in his award-winning book, Big House, Little House, Back House, Barn: The Connected Farm Buildings of New England, demonstrates that average farmers were, in fact, motivated by competition with farmers in other regions of America, who had better soils and growing seasons and fewer rocks to clear. Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Hill Library 1151 Parker Mtn Rd STRAFFORD NH 03884

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Humanities to GoMary AdamsThe Hill LibraryKen Berry, 603-815-1129

7:30 pm - 8:30 pm Toni Dove: Sunjammer 6: A Tale Blown by a Solar Breeze Daryl Roth Studio Theater

All dates: Sep 11, Sep 12

Fall into a poetic, cosmic experience with this interactive journey through time, guided by the ghost of a Hellenistic astronomer.

A luminary and innovator in interactive stories, Dove is known for genre-mashing works that employ responsive technologies to create visceral narrative experiences and explore the implications of technological change. Her latest work braids together choreography, a dreamlike movie and a responsive machine that adapts to viewer or performer input.

Get more info and tickets here.

Sep 11 Sep 12

Saturday, September 12, 2026

10:00am Duck Race Auburn Village 0

Thousands gather each September for a fun-filled family event hosted by the Auburn Historical Association. The Duck Race is the main attraction with prizes for the 10 fastest ducks, including a top prize of $1,000! There will be food trucks, local artisans and authors, a small petting farm, an apple pie contest, a kids baking contest, music and climbing wall. Active demonstrations of wool spinning and carding, cider pressing, blacksmithing, rug braiding, pottery making and so much more are ongoing continuously during the day. A parade to celebrate our country's 250th birthday will proceed down Hooksett Rd. 10 am to 3 pm in Auburn Village, Hooksett Road. Admission is free. For more info, visit www.auburnhistorical.org or contact us at info@auburnhistorical.org.

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.

Add to Google Calendar
1:00pm Late in Arriving, How Electricity Changed Rural New Hampshire Life East Grafton Union Church 80 Turnpike Rd Grafton NH 03240

Imagine a New Hampshire town where some people enjoyed the benefits of electricity – lighting at the flick of a switch and reliable heating controlled by a thermostat – while others lived with smelly kerosene lamps and smoky box stoves. In New Hampshire, during the first half of the 20th century,  residents of developed communities enjoyed the transformative benefits of electric power while those in the sparsely populated regions lived and worked in conditions little changed from the 19th century.  

It took the coming of the New Deal's Rural Electrification Administration and a determined band of farmers to overcome opposition from the established private utilities to create the New Hampshire Electric Cooperative in 1939. Despite labor and material shortages during World War II, within a decade, power came to almost all of the previously unserved areas of the state. For thousands of households, this meant relief from the drudgery of the wood-fueled cookstove, the washboard and the kerosene lamp. Reliable electric power similarly brought new ways of life to farmers and artisans. This program will explore how these developments changed civic and social life in New Hampshire’s countryside, and touch the ways the disparity of broadband access in the state today echoes these earlier struggles.

For handicap access, there is a ramp on the down-hill side of the building towards the back.
Humanities to GoStephen TaylorFriends of the Grafton LibraryMary Gasiorowski, mgasiorowski53@gmail.com

Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Historic Lighthouses and Keepers of New Hampshire & Southern Maine Madbury Town Hall 13 Town Hall Road Madbury NH 03823

Jeremy D'Entremont highlights the fascinating stories of the lighthouse keepers and their families who, beginning in 1771, mainainted the lighthouses between Portsmouth, New Hampshire, and York, Maine: Portsmouth Harbor Light, Whaleback Light, White Island (Isles of Shoals) Light, Boon Island Light, and Cape Neddick "Nubble" Light. The histories of these lighthouses and their keepers reveal rich human interest stories and fascinating lore, from rescues and heroism to isolation and tragedy, and even ghost stories. The program also explores ongoing efforts to preserve these iconic maritime sentinels today.

Humanities to GoJeremy D'EntremontMadbury Historical Society, Madbury Public LibraryElizabeth Carroll, 6037425131
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Lafayette and the Farewell Tour: Odyssey of an American Idol Camp Morgan Lodge 339 Millen Pond Road Washington New Hampshire 03280

In 1824-1825 the Marquis de Lafayette visisted all 24 states as the last surviving major general of the Continental Army. This Farewell Tour was described by contemporaries as a unique and extraordinary event. Edward Everett of Massachusetts called the Tour “an event taken in all its part, unparalleled in the history of man.” In this program, Alan Hoffman, translator of Lafayette in America in 1824 and 1825, a first-hand account of Lafayette’s Farewell Tour, will examine how Lafayette’s story-book life, role in the American Revolution, friendship with George Washington, support of American interests, and his Farewell Tour shaped his remarkable reputation. He will highlight Lafayette’s visits to cities and towns in New Hampshire to illustrate how the American people celebrated Lafayette and America during the Tour. 

Potluck at 6:00pm. Short Historical Society update meeting at 6:45pm
Humanities to GoAlan HoffmanWashington NH Historical SocietyStephen Yusko, millenpondnh@gmail.com

3 pm - 5 pm Student Job Fair Collis 107 Common Ground

The Student Employment Office hosts a Student Job Fair to assist students in finding employment opportunities while enrolled in classes. Employment will provide you with valuable professional experience as well as spending money!

Every student seeking employment is encouraged to attend. Bring your resume if you have one - it's worth your time.

Human Resources
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
3:30 pm - 5:00 pm The American Revolution and the Fate of the World Online - Zoom
When we think of the American Revolution, we picture 13 colonies squaring off against the British Crown in a spirited bid for independence. But this version of the story is only half the truth—and perhaps not even the most interesting half. In this riveting program, historian and author Richard Bell invites us to rediscover the Revolution as a world war that unleashed chaos, opportunity, and transformation across six continents—from the sugar fields of the Caribbean to the court of the King of Mysore, from refugee camps on the Canadian frontier to political uprisings in Sierra Leone and Peru—and traces the far-flung reverberations of the war through the lives of the people it displaced, empowered, or destroyed. Richard Bell is Professor of History at the University of Maryland and author of the book Stolen: Five Free Boys Kidnapped into Slavery and their Astonishing Odyssey Home which was a finalist for the George Washington Prize and the Harriet Tubman Prize. He has held major research fellowships at Yale, Cambridge, and the Library of Congress and is the recipient of the National Endowment of the Humanities Public Scholar award and the Andrew Carnegie Fellowship. His new book, The American Revolution and the Fate of the World, was published by Penguin in November 2025.

He maintains a list of upcoming events at Richard-Bell.com. Cost:Osher Members: $10
Non-Members: $15 Learn More and Register

Please note this is an online only lecture!
Osher Lifelong Learning Institute at Dartmouth
